sections.title_en: How after many years the Shaykh came from the desert to the city of Ghazna and carried round the basket in obedience to the behest from the Unseen and distributed amongst the poor all that was collected. “When any one possesses the spirit of the glory of Labbayka, letter on letter and messenger after messenger are,” as the window of a house is open,
sunbeams and moonbeams and rain and letters and so forth never cease coming in.

entries.farsi: رو به شهر آورد آن فرمان پذير / شهر غزنين گشت از رويش منير
entries.english: That obedient to the command turned his face towards the city; the city of Ghazna became illumined by his face.

entries.farsi: از فرح خلقى به استقبال رفت / ‏او در آمد از ره دزديده تفت
entries.english: A multitude joyfully went out to meet him; he entered in haste and furtively.

entries.farsi: جمله اعيان و مهان برخاستند / قصرها از بهر او آراستند
entries.english: All the notables and grandees rose up and made their palaces ready to receive him,

entries.farsi: گفت من از خود نمايى نامدم / ‏جز به خوارى و گدايى نامدم
entries.english: He said, “I do not come from self-advertisement: I come not save in humility and beggary.

entries.farsi: نيستم در عزم قال و قيل من / ‏در به در گردم به كف زنبيل من
entries.english: I have no intention of talking and discoursing: I will go about from door to door with a basket in my hand.

entries.farsi: بنده فرمانم كه امر است از خدا / كه گدا باشم گدا باشم گدا
entries.english: I am devoted to the edict, for it is commanded by God that I should be a beggar, a beggar, a beggar.

entries.farsi: تا شوم غرقه‏ى مذلت من تمام / ‏تا سقطها بشنوم از خاص و عام
entries.english: I will not use choice expressions in begging: I will tread the way of none but the vile beggars,

entries.farsi: در گدايى لفظ نادر ناورم / ‏جز طريق خس گدايان نسپرم
entries.english: That I may be completely overwhelmed with abasement, and that I may hear abusive words from high and low.

entries.farsi: امر حق جان است و من آن را تبع / ‏او طمع فرمود ذل من طمع
entries.english: God’s command is my soul, and I am its follower: He has commanded me to be covetous, base is he that covets.’

entries.farsi: چون طمع خواهد ز من سلطان دين / ‏خاك بر فرق قناعت بعد از اين
entries.english: Since the Sultan of the Judgement desires covetousness from me, dust on the head of contentment henceforth!

entries.farsi: او مذلت خواست كى عزت تنم / ‏او گدايى خواست كى ميرى كنم
entries.english: He has desired covetousness: how should I be ambitious of glory? He has desired beggary: how should I exercise sovereignty?

entries.farsi: بعد از اين كد و مذلت جان من / ‏بيست عباس‏اند در انبان من
entries.english: Henceforth beggary and abasement are my soul: in my wallet are twenty ‘Abbas.”

entries.farsi: شيخ بر مى‏گشت و زنبيلى به دست / ‏شى‏ء هلل خواجه توفيقيت هست
entries.english: The Shaykh would go about, with a basket in his hand, saying, “Give something, Sir, for God’s sake, if He prompts you.”

entries.farsi: انبيا هر يك همين فن مى‏زنند / خلق مفلس كديه ايشان مى‏كنند
entries.english: His inward experiences were higher than the Footstool and the Throne; his business was, “Something for God’s sake, something for God’s sake!”

entries.farsi: برتر از كرسى و عرش اسرار او / شى‏ء هلل شى‏ء هلل كار او
entries.english: The prophets, every one, ply this same trade: the people are destitute, they practise beggary,

entries.farsi: أَقْرَضُ وا اللهََّ أَقْرِضُ وا اللهََّ مى‏زنند / باژگون بر انصروا اللهَّ مى‏تنند
entries.english: Crying, “Lend to God, lend to God,” and persevering contrarily in “Help God!”

entries.farsi: دربدر اين شيخ مى‏آرد نياز / بر فلك صد در براى شيخ باز
entries.english: This Shaykh is going as a suppliant from door to door, in Heaven a hundred doors are opened for the Shaykh,

entries.farsi: كان گدايى كان به جد مى‏كرد او / بهر يزدان بود نز بهر گلو
entries.english: Because the beggary that he practiced diligently was for the sake of God, not for the sake of his gullet;

entries.farsi: ور بكردى نيز از بهر گلو / آن گلو از نور حق دارد غلو
entries.english: And even if he had done it for the sake of his gullet, that gullet has exorbitance by the Light of God.

entries.farsi: در حق او خورد نان و شهد و شير / به ز چله وز سه روزه‏ى صد فقير
entries.english: As regards him, the eating of bread and honey and the drinking of milk is better than the forty days’ seclusion and the three days’ fast of a hundred dervishes.

entries.farsi: نور مى‏نوشد مگو نان مى‏خورد / الله مى‏كارد به صورت مى‏چرد
entries.english: He eats Light; do not say he eats bread: he sows anemones in appearance he feeds.

entries.farsi: چون شرارى كاو خورد روغن ز شمع / ‏نور افزايد ز خوردش بهر جمع
entries.english: Like the flame that consumes the oil in a candle, from his eating and drinking there is an increase of light for the company.

entries.farsi: نان خورى را گفت حق ال تسرفوا / نور خوردن را نگفته‏ست اكتفوا
entries.english: God has said, “Be not immoderate,” in reference to the eating of bread; He has not said, “Be satisfied,” in reference to the eating of Light.

entries.farsi: آن گلوى ابتال بد وين گلو / فارغ از اسراف و ايمن از غلو
entries.english: The former was the gullet subject to probation, while this gullet was free from immoderation and secure from exorbitance.

entries.farsi: امر و فرمان بود نه حرص و طمع / ‏آن چنان جان حرص را نبود تبع
entries.english: It was the command and order, not greed and cupidity: a spirit like that is not a follower of greed.

entries.farsi: گر بگويد كيميا مس را بده / ‏تو به من خود را طمع نبود فره
entries.english: If the elixir say to the copper, “Give yourself up to me,” cupidity does not prevail.

entries.farsi: گنجهاى خاك تا هفتم طبق / ‏عرضه كرده بود پيش شيخ حق
entries.english: God had offered to the Shaykh the treasures of the earth down to the seventh tier;

entries.farsi: شيخ گفتا خالقا من عاشقم / ‏گر بجويم غير تو من فاسقم
entries.english: The Shaykh said, “O Creator, I am a lover: if I seek aught but You, I am impious.

entries.farsi: هشت جنت گر در آرم در نظر / ور كنم خدمت من از خوف سقر
entries.english: If I should bring into view the Eight Paradises, or if I should serve You from fear of Hell,

entries.farsi: مومنى باشم سالمت جوى من / ‏ز انكه اين هر دو بود حظ بدن
entries.english: I am a believer seeking salvation, for both these are concerned with the body.”

entries.farsi: عاشقى كز عشق يزدان خورد قوت / ‏صد بدن پيشش نيرزد تره توت
entries.english: A hundred bodies are not worth a bean in the eyes of the lover who has received nutriment from God’s love;

entries.farsi: وين بدن كه دارد آن شيخ فطن / ‏چيز ديگر گشت كم خوانش بدن
entries.english: And this body which the Shaykh of insight possesses has become something different: do not call it a body.

entries.farsi: عاشق عشق خدا و آن گاه مزد / جبرئيل موتمن و آن گاه دزد
entries.english: In love with God’s love and then a wage! A trusted Gabriel and then a thief!

entries.farsi: عاشق آن ليلى كور و كبود / ملك عالم پيش او يك تره بود
entries.english: In the eyes of that wretched lover of Layla the kingdom of the world was a vegetable.

entries.farsi: پيش او يكسان شده بد خاك و زر / زر چه باشد كه نبد جان را خطر
entries.english: Earth and gold were alike in his eyes. What of gold? His life had no value.

entries.farsi: شير و گرگ و دد از او واقف شده / ‏همچو خويشان گرد او گرد آمده
entries.english: Lions and wolves and wild beasts were acquainted with him and gathered round him like kinsfolk,

entries.farsi: كاين شده‏ست از خوى حيوان پاك پاك / ‏پر ز عشق و لحم و شحمش زهرناك
entries.english: That this man had become entirely purged of animality and filled with love and that his flesh and fat were poisonous.

entries.farsi: زهر دد باشد شكر ريز خرد / ز انكه نيك نيك باشد ضد بد
entries.english: The sweets scattered by Reason are poison to the wild beast, because the good of good is antagonistic to evil.

entries.farsi: لحم عاشق را نيارد خورد دد / عشق معروف است پيش نيك و بد
entries.english: The wild beast dare not devour the flesh of the lover: Love is known both to the good and the evil;

entries.farsi: ور خورد خود فى المثل دام و ددش / ‏گوشت عاشق زهر گردد بكشدش
entries.english: And if the wild beast devours him even metaphorically, the lover’s flesh will become poison and kill him.

entries.farsi: هر چه جز عشق است شد مأكول عشق / ‏دو جهان يك دانه پيش نول عشق
entries.english: Everything except love is devoured by Love: to the beak of Love the two worlds are a single grain.

entries.farsi: دانه‏اى مر مرغ را هرگز خورد / كاهدان مر اسب را هرگز چرد
entries.english: Does a grain ever devour the bird? Does the manger ever feed on the horse?

entries.farsi: بندگى كن تا شوى عاشق لعل / ‏بندگى كسبى است آيد در عمل
entries.english: Do service, that perchance you may become a lover: service is a means of gaining Love: it comes into action.

entries.farsi: بنده آزادى طمع دارد ز جد / عاشق آزادى نخواهد تا ابد
entries.english: The servant desires to be freed from Fortune; the lover nevermore desires to be free.

entries.farsi: بنده دايم خلعت و ادرار جوست / ‏خلعت عاشق همه ديدار دوست
entries.english: The servant is always seeking a robe of honour and a stipend; the entire lover’s robe of honour is his vision of the Beloved.

entries.farsi: در نگنجد عشق در گفت و شنيد / عشق دريايى است قعرش ناپديد
entries.english: Love is not contained in speech and hearing: Love is an ocean whereof the depth is invisible.

entries.farsi: قطره‏هاى بحر را نتوان شمرد / هفت دريا پيش آن بحر است خرد
entries.english: The drops of the sea cannot be numbered: the Seven Seas are petty in comparison with that Ocean.

entries.farsi: اين سخن پايان ندارد اى فالن / باز رو در قصه‏ى شيخ زمان
entries.english: This discourse has no end. Return, O reader, to the story of the Shaykh of the time.

sections.title_fa: ‏در معنى لوالك لما خلقت الافلاک
sections.title_en: On the meaning of “But for you, I would not have created the heavens.”

entries.farsi: شد چنين شيخى گدايى كو به كو / عشق آمد ال ابالى اتقوا
entries.english: A Shaykh like this became a beggar from street to street. Love is reckless: beware!

entries.farsi: عشق بشكافد فلك را صد شكاف / ‏عشق لرزاند زمين را از گزاف
entries.english: Love makes the sea boil like a kettle; Love crumbles the mountain like sand;

entries.farsi: عشق جوشد بحر را مانند ديگ / ‏عشق سايد كوه را مانند ريگ
entries.english: Love cleaves the sky with a hundred clefts; Love unconscionably makes the earth to tremble.

entries.farsi: با محمد بود عشق پاك جفت / ‏بهر عشق او را خدا لوالك گفت
entries.english: The pure Love was united with Mohammed: for Love’s sake God said to him, “But for you.”

entries.farsi: منتهى در عشق چون او بود فرد / پس مر او را ز انبيا تخصيص كرد
entries.english: Since he alone was the ultimate goal in Love, therefore God singled him out from the prophets,

entries.farsi: گر نبودى بهر عشق پاك را / كى وجودى دادمى افلاک را
entries.english: “Had it not been for pure Love’s sake, how should I have bestowed an existence on the heavens?

entries.farsi: من بدان افراشتم چرخ سنى / ‏تا علو عشق را فهمى كنى
entries.english: I have lifted up the lofty celestial sphere, that you may apprehend the sublimity of Love.

entries.farsi: منفعتهاى دگر آيد ز چرخ / ‏آن چو بيضه تابع آيد اين چو فرخ
entries.english: Other benefits come from the celestial sphere: it is like the egg, these are consequential, like the chick.

entries.farsi: خاك را من خوار كردم يك سرى / ‏تا ز خوارى عاشقان بويى برى
entries.english: I have made the earth altogether lowly, that you may gain some notion of the lowliness of lovers.

entries.farsi: با تو گويند اين جبال راسيات / ‏وصف حال عاشقان اندر ثبات
entries.english: We have given greenness and freshness to the earth, that you may become acquainted with the transmutation of the dervish.”

entries.farsi: خاك را داديم سبزى و نوى / ‏تا ز تبديل فقير آگه شوى
entries.english: These firm-set mountains describe to you the state of lovers in steadfastness,

entries.farsi: گر چه آن معنى است و اين نقش اى پسر / تا به فهم تو كند نزديك‏تر
entries.english: Although that is a reality, while this is an image, O son, in order that he may bring it nearer to your understanding.

entries.farsi: غصه را با خار تشبيهى كنند / آن نباشد ليك تنبيهى كنند
entries.english: They liken anguish to thorns; it is not that, but they do so as a means of arousing attention.

entries.farsi: آن دل قاسى كه سنگش خواندند / نامناسب بد مثالى راندند
entries.english: When they called a hard heart “stony,” that was inappropriate, they made it serve as a similitude.

entries.farsi: در تصور در نيايد عين آن / ‏عيب بر تصوير نه نفيش مدان
entries.english: The archetype of that is inconceivable: put the blame on your conceptual faculty, and do not regard it as nonexistent.

sections.title_fa: رفتن آن شيخ در خانه‏ى اميرى بهر كديه روزى چهار بار با زنبيل به اشارت غيب و عتاب كردن امير او را بدان وقاحت و عذر گفتن او امير را
sections.title_en: How the Shaykh, in obedience to the intimation from the Unseen, went with his basket
four times in one day to the house of a certain Amír for the purpose of begging; and how the Amír rebuked him for his impudence, and how he excused himself to the Amír.

entries.farsi: شيخ روزى چار كرت چون فقير / بهر كديه رفت در قصر امير
entries.english: One day the Shaykh went four times to the palace of an Amír, in order to beg like a dervish,

entries.farsi: در كفش زنبيل و شى‏ء هلل زنان / ‏خالق جان مى‏بجويد تاى نان
entries.english: A basket in his hand, crying, “Something for God’s sake! The Creator of the soul is seeking a piece of bread.”

entries.farsi: نعلهاى باژگونه‏ست اى پسر / عقل كلى را كند هم خيره سر
entries.english: It is preposterous, O son: it makes even Universal Reason giddy-headed.

entries.farsi: چون اميرش ديد گفتش اى وقيح / ‏گويمت چيزى منه نامم شحيح
entries.english: When the Amír saw him, he said to him, “O impudent man, I will tell you something, do not fasten on me the name of miser.

entries.farsi: اين چه سغرى و چه روى است و چه كار / كه به روزى اندر آيى چار بار
entries.english: What callousness and effrontery and behaviour is this that you come in four times in one day?

entries.farsi: حرمت و آب گدايان برده‏اى / ‏اين چه عباسى زشت آورده‏اى
entries.english: Who here is attached to you, Shaykh? Never have I seen a sturdy beggar like you.

entries.farsi: كيست اينجا شيخ اندر بند تو / من نديدم نر گدا مانند تو
entries.english: You have brought beggars into contempt and disgrace: what abominable importunity, worthy of Abbas, is this that you have shown!

entries.farsi: غاشيه بر دوش تو عباس دبس / ‏هيچ ملحد را مباد اين نفس نحس
entries.english: Abbas, the seller of date-syrup is your groom: may no freethinker (mulhid) have such an ill-starred soul!”

entries.farsi: گفت اميرا بنده فرمانم خموش / ‏ز آتشم آگه نه‏اى چندين مجوش
entries.english: He replied, “O Amír, I am devoted to the command. Be silent! You are not acquainted with my fire: do not boil so much!

entries.farsi: بهر نان در خويش حرصى ديدمى / ‏اشكم نان خواه را بدريدمى
entries.english: Had I found in myself any greed for bread, I would have ripped my bread craving belly.

entries.farsi: هفت سال از سوز عشق جسم پز / در بيابان خورده‏ام من برگ رز
entries.english: During seven years, by the ardour of Love that cooks the body, I have eaten vine-leaves in the wilderness,

entries.farsi: تا تو باشى در حجاب بو البشر / سرسرى در عاشقان كمتر نگر
entries.english: So that, from my eating withered and fresh leaves, this bodily colour of mine had turned green.”

entries.farsi: تا ز برگ خشك و تازه خوردنم / ‏سبز گشته بود اين رنگ تنم
entries.english: So long as you art in the veil of the Father of mankind, do not look slightingly on the lovers.

entries.farsi: زيركان كه مويها بشكافتند / علم هيات را به جان دريافتند
entries.english: The acute men who have split hairs and with their soul have apprehended the science of astronomy,

entries.farsi: علم نيرنجات و سحر و فلسفه / ‏گر چه نشناسند حق المعرفه
entries.english: And the sciences of sorcery and magic and philosophy, and, though they do not know with real knowledge,

entries.farsi: ليك كوشيدند تا امكان خود / بر گذشتند از همه اقران خود
entries.english: Yet have endeavoured as far as they possibly can, and have surpassed all their rivals—

entries.farsi: عشق غيرت كرد و ز يشان در كشيد / شد چنين خورشيد ز يشان ناپديد
entries.english: Love was jealous and withdrew from them: such a Sun became invisible to them.

entries.farsi: زين گذر كن پند من بپذير هين / ‏عاشقان را تو به چشم عشق بين
entries.english: How did such a Sun withdraw its face from the light of an eye that observed a star in the daytime?

entries.farsi: نور چشمى كاو به روز استاره ديد / آفتابى چون از او رو در كشيد
entries.english: Abandon this; listen, accept my counsel: regard the lovers with the eye of love.

entries.farsi: وقت نازك باشد و جان در رصد / با تو نتوان گفت آن دم عذر خود
entries.english: Their time is precious and their souls are on the watch for the Beloved: at that moment they cannot excuse themselves to you.

entries.farsi: فهم كن موقوف آن گفتن مباش / ‏سينه‏هاى عاشقان را كم خراش
entries.english: Apprehend their real state, do not be dependent on their words, and do not wound the breasts of the lovers.

entries.farsi: نه گمانى برده‏اى تو زين نشاط / حزم را مگذار مى‏كن احتياط
entries.english: Have not you formed a bad opinion of this enthusiasm? Do not abandon prudence, always act with caution;

entries.farsi: واجب است و جايز است و مستحيل / ‏اين وسط را گير در حزم اى دخيل
entries.english: It is either necessary or allowable or absurd: take this middle course in prudence, O interferer.

sections.title_fa: گريان شدن امير از نصيحت شيخ و عكس صدق او و ايثار كردن مخزن بعد از آن گستاخى و ‏استعصام شيخ و قبول ناكردن و گفتن كه من بى‏اشارتى نيارم تصرفى كردن
sections.title_en: How the admonition of the Shaykh and the reflection of his sincerity moved the Amír to weep;
and how after that irreverence he gave up his treasury; and how the Shaykh preserved himself and refused to accept and said, “I cannot take any action in the absence of intimation.”

entries.farsi: اين بگفت و گريه در شد هاى هاى / ‏اشك غلطان بر رخ او جاى جاى
entries.english: He said this and began to weep with ecstatic cries, the tears rolling hither and there down his cheeks.

entries.farsi: صدق او هم بر ضمير مير زد / عشق هر دم طرفه ديگى مى‏پزد
entries.english: His sincerity touched the Amír’s heart: Love is ever cooking a wondrous pot full.

entries.farsi: صدق عاشق بر جمادى مى‏تند / چه عجب گر بر دل دانا زند
entries.english: The sincerity of the lover affects an inanimate thing: what wonder if it makes an impression on the mind of one possessed of knowledge?

entries.farsi: صدق احمد بر جمال ماه زد / بلكه بر خورشيد رخشان راه زد
entries.english: The sincerity of Moses made an impression on the rod and the mountain; no, on the majestic sea.

entries.farsi: صدق موسى بر عصا و كوه زد / بلكه بر درياى پر اشكوه زد
entries.english: The sincerity of Ahmad made an impression on the beauty of the moon; no, it stopped the course of the shining sun.

entries.farsi: رو به رو آورده هر دو در نفير / گشته گريان هم امير و هم فقير
entries.english: With face turned to face in lamentation, both the Amír and the Dervish had fallen to weeping.

entries.farsi: ساعتى بسيار چون بگريستند / گفت مير او را كه خيز اى ارجمند
entries.english: After they had wept much for a while, the Amír said to him, “Arise, O worthy man,

entries.farsi: خانه آن تست هر چت ميل هست / ‏بر گزين خود هر دو عالم اندك است
entries.english: And choose from the Treasury whatever you will, albeit you deserve a hundred such.

entries.farsi: هر چه خواهى از خزانه بر گزين / ‏گر چه استحقاق دارى صد چنين
entries.english: The house is yours: choose anything you desire, in truth the two worlds are little.”

entries.farsi: گفت دستورى ندادندم چنين / ‏كه به دست خويش چيزى بر گزين
entries.english: He replied, “I have not been given permission to pick out anything with my own hand.

entries.farsi: من ز خود نتوانم اين كردن فضول / ‏كه كنم من اين دخيالنه دخول
entries.english: I cannot of my own accord commit such impertinence as to intrude in this way like an interloper.”

entries.farsi: اين بهانه كرد و مهره در ربود / مانع آن بد كان عطا صادق نبود
entries.english: He made this excuse and took his leave: what prevented was (the fact) that the munificence was not sincere.

entries.farsi: نه كه صادق بود و پاك از غل و خشم / ‏شيخ را هر صدق مى‏نامد به چشم
entries.english: Was it not that it was sincere and unmixed with rancour and wrath? Every type of sincerity did not come into the Shaykh’s consideration.

entries.farsi: گفت فرمانم چنين داده ست اله / ‏كه گدايانه برو نانى بخواه
entries.english: He said, “God has so commanded me, saying, ‘Go as a beggar and ask for a piece of bread.’”

sections.title_fa: اشارت آمدن از غيب به شيخ كه اين دو سال به فرمان ما بستدى و بدادى بعد از اين بده و مستان دست در زير حصير مى‏كن كه آن را چون انبان بو هريره كرديم در حق تو هر چه خواهى بيابى تا يقين شود عالميان را كه وراى اين عالمى است كه خاك به كف گيرى زر شود مرده در او آيد زنده شود نحس اكبر در وى آيد سعد اكبر شود كفر در او آيد ايمان گردد زهر در او آيد ترياق شود، نه ،داخل اين عالم است و نه خارج اين عالم نه تحت و نه فوق نه متصل نه منفصل بى‏چون و بى‏چگونه
هر دم از او هزاران اثر و نمونه ظاهر مى‏شود، چنان كه صنعت دست با صورت دست و غمزه‏ى چشم با صورت چشم و فصاحت زبان با صورت زبان نه داخل است و نه خارج او نه متصل و نه ‏منفصل، و العاقل يكفيه الاشاره

sections.title_en: How the intimation came to the Shaykh from the Unseen: “During these two years you have taken and given by Our command; henceforth give but do not take; always put your hand under the mat which on your behalf We have made to be like the wallet of Abu Hurayra, and you will find whatever you may desire.” that the people of the world may gain certainty that beyond this is a world where, if you take a handful of earth, it will turn to gold; if a dead man enter it he will become living; if the

most ill-starred enter it he will become the most fortunate; if infidelity enters there, it will become faith; if poison enter therein, it will become an antidote. It is neither inside of this world nor outside; neither beneath it nor above it; neither joined with it nor separate from it: it is devoid of quality and relation. At every moment thousands of signs and types are displayed by it. As manual skill to the

form of the hand, or glances of the eye to the form of the eye, or eloquence of the tongue to the form of the tongue,: it is neither inside of it nor outside, neither joined with it nor separate. And indication is sufficient for a person of intelligence.

entries.farsi: تا دو سال اين كار كرد آن مرد كار / بعد از آن امر آمدش از كردگار
entries.english: For two years that man of accomplishment carried on this business; after that the command came to him from the Creator—

entries.farsi: بعد از اين مى ده ولى از كس مخواه / ‏ما بداديمت ز غيب اين دستگاه
entries.english: “Henceforth continue to give, but do not beg from any one: We from the Unseen World have bestowed on you this power.

entries.farsi: هر كه خواهد از تو از يك تا هزار / دست در زير حصيرى كن بر آر
entries.english: Whoever begs of you, from one to a thousand, put your hand beneath a mat and produce.

entries.farsi: هين ز گنج رحمت بى‏مر بده / ‏در كف تو خاك گردد زر بده
entries.english: Listen, give from the incalculable treasure of Divine mercy: in your hand earth will become gold: give!

entries.farsi: هر چه خواهندت بده منديش از آن / داد يزدان را تو بيش از بيش دان
entries.english: Give whatever they ask of you: have no anxiety as to that: know that the bounty of God is more than more.

entries.farsi: در عطاى ما نه تحشير و نه كم / ‏نه پشيمانى نه حسرت زين كرم
entries.english: In Our bounty there is no retrenchment or reduction; no sorrow or regret for this generosity.

entries.farsi: دست زير بوريا كن اى سند / از براى روى پوش چشم بد
entries.english: Put your hand beneath the mat, O trusted man, in order to blindfold the evil eye.

entries.farsi: پس ز زير بوريا پر كن تو مشت / ‏ده به دست سائل بشكسته پشت
entries.english: Fill your fist, therefore, from beneath the mat and give into the hand of the beggar whose back is broken.

entries.farsi: بعد از اين از اجر ناممنون بده / ‏هر كه خواهد گوهر مكنون بده
entries.english: Henceforth give from the wage that is not grudged: give the hidden pearl to everyone who desires it.

entries.farsi: رو يَدُ اللهَِّ فَوْقَ أَيْدِيهِمْ تو باش / همچو دست حق گزافى رزق پاش
entries.english: Go; be the Hand of God is above their hands: do you, like the Hand of God, scatter the daily bread recklessly.

entries.farsi: وام داران را ز عهده وا رهان / ‏همچو باران سبز كن فرش جهان
entries.english: Release those in debt from their responsibility: like rain, make the carpet of the world green.”

entries.farsi: بود يك سال دگر كارش همين / ‏كه بدادى زر ز كيسه‏ى رب دين
entries.english: During another year this was his work, that he was always giving gold from the purse of the Lord of the Judgement.

entries.farsi: زر شدى خاك سيه اندر كفش / ‏حاتم طايى گدايى در صفش
entries.english: The black earth turned into gold in his hand: beside him Hatím of Tayyi was a beggar.

sections.title_fa: دانستن شيخ ضمير سائل را بى‏گفتن و دانستن قدر وام وام داران بى‏گفتن كه نشان آن باشد كه اخرج ‏بصفاتى الى خلقى
sections.title_en: How the Shaykh knew the unspoken thoughts of those who begged of him and the sums owed by the debtors without their telling him, which is a sign of,
“Go forth with My attributes unto My creatures.”

entries.farsi: حاجت خود گر نگفتى آن فقير / او بدادى و بدانستى ضمير
entries.english: If a dervish said nothing about his need, he would give and would know his secret thought;

entries.farsi: آن چه در دل داشتى آن پشت خم / ‏قدر آن دادى بدو نه بيش و كم
entries.english: He would give that bent-backed one the amount that he had in mind, neither more nor less.

entries.farsi: پس بگفتندى چه دانستى كه او / اين قدر انديشه دارد اى عمو
entries.english: Then they would ask, “How did you know, uncle that he was thinking of this amount?”

entries.farsi: او بگفتى خانه‏ى دل خلوت است / ‏خالى از كديه مثال جنت است
entries.english: He would reply: “My heart’s house is empty: it is void of beggary, like Paradise.

entries.farsi: اندر او جز عشق يزدان كار نيست / ‏جز خيال وصل او ديار نيست
entries.english: There is no work in it except love of God: there is no inhabitant except the idea of union with Him.

entries.farsi: خانه را من روفتم از نيك و بد / خانه‏ام پرست از عشق احد
entries.english: I have swept the house clean of good and evil: my house is filled with love of the One.

entries.farsi: هر چه بينم اندر او غير خدا / آن من نبود بود عكس گدا
entries.english: When I see in it anything other than God, it is not mine but is reflected from the beggar.”

entries.farsi: گر در آيى نخل يا عرجون نمود / جز ز عكس نخله‏ى بيرون نبود
entries.english: If a date-palm or a cluster of dates has appeared in a piece of water, it is only the reflection from the tree outside.

entries.farsi: در تگ آب ار ببينى صورتى / ‏عكس بيرون باشد آن نقش اى فتى
entries.english: If you see a form at the bottom of the water, that image is reflected from outside, O youth;

entries.farsi: ليك تا آب از قذى خالى‏شدن / ‏تنقيه شرط است در جوى بدن
entries.english: But it is necessary to cleanse the canal, the body, until the water is cleared of scum,

entries.farsi: تا نماند تيرگى و خس در او / تا امين گردد نمايد عكس رو
entries.english: In order that no obscurity and rubbish may remain therein and that it may become trustworthy and that the reflection of the aspect may appear.

entries.farsi: جز گالبه در تنت كو اى مقل / ‏آب صافى كن ز گل اى خصم دل
entries.english: Where in your body is aught but muddy water, O you who are destitute? Make the water pure from mud, O enemy of the heart.

entries.farsi: تو بر آنى هر دمى كز خواب‏و خور / خاك ريزى اندر اين جو بيشتر
entries.english: By sleeping and eating and drinking you are ever intent on pouring into this canal more earth.

sections.title_fa: ‏سبب دانستن ضميرهاى خلق
sections.title_en: The means of knowing people’s hidden thoughts.

entries.farsi: چون دل آن آب زينها خالى است / ‏عكس روها از برون در آب جست
entries.english: When the heart of that water is void of these, does the reflection of the aspects dart into the water.

entries.farsi: پس ترا باطن مصفا ناشده / ‏خانه پر از ديو و نسناس و دده
entries.english: Therefore, unless your interior has been purified, the house is full of demons and monsters and wild beasts,

entries.farsi: اى خرى ز استيزه مانده در خرى / ‏كى ز ارواح مسيحى بو برى
entries.english: O ass that has obstinately remained in asininity, how will you get scent of the breaths which resemble those of the Messiah?

entries.farsi: كى شناسى گر خيالى سر كند / كز كدامين مكمنى سر بر كند
entries.english: If a phantasy appears, how will you know from what hiding-place it springs forth?

entries.farsi: چون خيالى مى‏شود در زهد تن / ‏تا خيالات از درونه روفتن
entries.english: Before phantasies are swept from the inward part, the body will become as a phantasy in renunciation.

sections.title_fa: غالب شدن مكر روبه بر استعصام خر
sections.title_en: How the cunning of the fox prevailed over the attempt of the ass to preserve himself from falling into temptation.

entries.farsi: خر بسى كوشيد و او را دفع گفت / ‏ليك جوع الكلب با خر بود جفت
entries.english: The ass strove long and argued against him, but ravenous hunger never quitted the ass.

entries.farsi: غالب آمد حرص و صبرش بد ضعيف / ‏بس گلوها كه برد عشق رغيف
entries.english: Greed prevailed, and his self-restraint was weak: many are the gullets that are cut by love of the loaf.

entries.farsi: ز آن رسولى كش حقايق داد دست / ‏كاد فقر ان يكون كفر آمده‏ست
entries.english: From the Messenger to whom the realities revealed themselves has come down, “Poverty is near being infidelity.”

entries.farsi: گشته بود آن خر مجاعت را اسير / گفت اگر مكر است يك ره مرده گير
entries.english: The ass had been made prisoner by hunger: he said, “If it is a plot, what then? Suppose I am dead once and for all,

entries.farsi: زين عذاب جوع بارى وا رهم / ‏گر حيات اين است من مرده به‏ام
entries.english: At any rate I shall be delivered from this torment of hunger: if this is life, I am better dead.”

entries.farsi: گر خر اول توبه و سوگند خورد / عاقبت هم از خرى خبطى بكرد
entries.english: If at first the ass repented and swore, in the end, because of his asininity, he made a lapse.

entries.farsi: حرص كور و احمق و نادان كند / مرگ را بر احمقان آسان كند
entries.english: Greed makes one blind and foolish and ignorant: to fools it makes death easy;

entries.farsi: نيست آسان مرگ بر جان خران / ‏كه ندارند آب جان جاودان
entries.english: Death is not easy to the souls of asses who do not possess the splendour of the everlasting soul.

entries.farsi: چون ندارد جان جاويد او شقى است / ‏جرات او بر اجل از احمقى است
entries.english: Since the ass does not possess the everlasting soul, he is damned: his boldness in death is the result of folly.

entries.farsi: جهد كن تا جان مخلد گرددت / تا به روز مرگ برگى باشدت
entries.english: Endeavour that your soul may become immortal, so that on the day of death you will have a store.

entries.farsi: اعتمادش نيز بر رازق نبود / كه بر افشاند بر او از غيب جود
entries.english: Again, he had no confidence in the Provider that He would scatter over him largesse from the Unseen.

entries.farsi: تا كنونش فضل بى‏روزى نداشت / ‏گر چه گه گه بر تنش جوعى گماشت
entries.english: Until now, the Divine Bounty had not kept him without the daily provision, though at times He subjected his body to a hunger.

entries.farsi: گر نباشد جوع صد رنج دگر / از پى هيضه بر آرد از تو سر
entries.english: Were hunger absent, in consequence of indigestion a hundred other afflictions would raise their heads in you.

entries.farsi: رنج جوع اولى بود خود ز آن علل / ‏هم به لطف و هم به خفت هم عمل
entries.english: Truly the affliction of hunger is better than those maladies in respect both of its subtlety and its lightness and work.

entries.farsi: رنج جوع از رنجها پاكيزه‏تر / خاصه در جوع است صد نفع و هنر
entries.english: The affliction of hunger is purer than afflictions, especially in hunger there are a hundred advantages and excellences.

sections.title_fa: ‏در بيان فضيلت احتما و جوع
sections.title_en: Explaining the excellence of abstinence and hunger

entries.farsi: جوع خود سلطان داروهاست هين / ‏جوع در جان نه چنين خوارش مبين
entries.english: Indeed hunger is the king of medicines: listen, lay hunger to your heart, and do not regard it with such contempt.

entries.farsi: جمله ناخوش از مجاعت خوش شده‏ست / ‏جمله خوشها بى‏مجاعتها رد است
entries.english: Everything sour is made sweet by hunger: without hunger all sweet things are unacceptable.

sections.title_en: Parable.

entries.farsi: آن يكى مى‏خورد نان فخفره / ‏گفت سائل چون بدين استت شره
entries.english: A certain person was eating bread made of bran: someone asked him, “How are you so fond of this?”

entries.farsi: گفت جوع از صبر چون دو تا شود / نان جو در پيش من حلوا شود
entries.english: He replied, “When hunger is doubled by self-denial, barley bread is halwá in my opinion;

entries.farsi: پس توانم كه همه حلوا خورم / ‏چون كنم صبرى صبورم ال جرم
entries.english: Therefore when I deny myself once, I can eat halwá entirely, of course I am very self-denying.”

entries.farsi: خود نباشد جوع هر كس را زبون / ‏كاين علف زارى است ز اندازه برون
entries.english: Hunger, in truth, is not conquered by everyone, for this is a place where fodder is abundant beyond measure.

entries.farsi: جوع مر خاصان حق را داده‏اند / تا شوند از جوع شير زورمند
entries.english: Hunger is bestowed as a gift on God’s elect, that through hunger they may become puissant lions.

entries.farsi: جوع هر جلف گدا را كى دهند / چون علف كم نيست پيش او نهند
entries.english: How should hunger be bestowed on every beggarly churl? Since the fodder is not scarce they set it before him,

entries.farsi: كه بخور كه هم بدين ارزانيى / ‏تو نه اى مرغاب مرغ نانيى
entries.english: Saying, “Eat! This is all you are worth: you are not a waterfowl, you are a bread-fowl.”

sections.title_fa: حكايت مريدى كه شيخ از حرص و ضمير او واقف شد او را نصيحت كرد به زبان و در ضمن ‏نصيحت قوت توكل بخشيدش به امر حق
sections.title_en: Story of the disciple of whose greediness and secret thoughts his Shaykh became aware. He admonished him with his tongue and in the course of his admonition bestowed on him,
by Divine command, the food of trust in God.

entries.farsi: شيخ مى‏شد با مريدى بى‏درنگ / ‏سوى شهرى نان در آن جا بود تنگ
entries.english: The Shaykh, accompanied by a disciple, was going without delay towards a certain town where bread was scarce,

entries.farsi: ترس جوع و قحط در فكر مريد / هر دمى مى‏گشت از غفلت پديد
entries.english: And the dread of hunger and famine was continually presenting itself to the disciple’s mind on account of his heedlessness.

entries.farsi: شيخ آگه بود و واقف از ضمير / گفت او را چند باشى در زحير
entries.english: The Shaykh was aware and acquainted with his secret thoughts: he said to him, “How long will you remain in torment?

entries.farsi: از براى غصه‏ى نان سوختى / ‏ديده‏ى صبر و توكل دوختى
entries.english: You are consumed because of your craving for bread: you have closed the eye of self-denial and trust in God.

entries.farsi: تو نه‏اى ز آن نازنينان عزيز / كه ترا دارند بى‏جوز و مويز
entries.english: You are not of the honoured favourites that you should be kept without walnuts and raisins.

entries.farsi: جوع رزق جان خاصان خداست / ‏كى زبون همچو تو گيج گداست
entries.english: Hunger is the daily bread of the souls of God’s elect: how is it amenable to a beggarly fool like you?

entries.farsi: باش فارغ تو از آنها نيستى / ‏كه در اين مطبخ تو بى‏نان بيستى
entries.english: Be at ease: you are not of those, so that you should tarry without bread in this kitchen.”

entries.farsi: كاسه بر كاسه‏ست و نان بر نان مدام / ‏از براى اين شكم خواران عام
entries.english: There are always bowls on bowls and loaves on loaves for these vulgar belly-gods.

entries.farsi: چون بميرد مى‏رود نان پيش پيش / ‏كاى ز بيم بى‏نوايى كشته خويش
entries.english: When he dies, the bread comes forward, saying, “O you who did kill yourself from fear of having no food,

entries.farsi: تو برفتى ماند نان بر خيز گير / اى بكشته خويش را اندر زحير
entries.english: You are gone, the bread is still there: arise and take it, O you who did kill yourself in agony!”

entries.farsi: هين توكل كن ملرزان پا و دست / ‏رزق تو بر تو ز تو عاشق‏تر است
entries.english: Listen, put trust in God, and do not let your feet and hands tremble: your daily bread is more in love with you than you with it.

entries.farsi: عاشق است و مى‏زند او مول مول / ‏كه ز بى‏صبريت داند اى فضول
entries.english: It is in love with you and is lingering because it knows of your lack of self-denial, O trifler.

entries.farsi: گر ترا صبرى بدى رزق آمدى / ‏خويشتن چون عاشقان بر تو زدى
entries.english: If you had any self-denial, the daily bread would come and throw itself upon you as lovers do.

entries.farsi: اين تب لرزه ز خوف جوع چيست / ‏در توكل سير مى‏تانند زيست
entries.english: What is this feverish trembling for fear of hunger? In trust in God one can live full-fed.

sections.title_fa: حكايت آن گاو كه تنها در جزيره اى است بزرگ، حق تعالى آن جزيره‏ى بزرگ پر كند از نبات و رياحين كه علف گاو باشد تا بشب آن گاو همه را بخورد و فربه شود چون كوه پاره اى، چون شب شود خوابش نبرد از غصه و خوف كه همه صحرا را چريدم فردا چه خورم تا از اين غصه الغر شود همچون خالل، روز بر خيزد همه صحرا را سبزتر و انبوه تر بيند از دى باز بخورد و فربه
شود، باز شبش همان غم بگيرد، سالهاست كه او همچنين مى‏بيند و اعتماد نمى‏كند
sections.title_en: Story of the cow that is alone in a great island. God most High fills the great island with plants and sweet herbs which are cows’ fodder, and the cow feeds on all that till nightfall and grows fat

as a mountain-crag. When night comes, she cannot sleep for anxiety and fear, “I have fed on the whole field: what shall I eat to-morrow?” So in consequence of this anxiety she becomes thin like a toothpick. At daybreak she sees the whole field is greener and richer than it was yesterday, and again she eats and grows fat. Then again at nightfall the same anxiety
seizes her. For years she has been experiencing the like of this, and she puts no confidence.

entries.farsi: يك جزيره‏ى سبز هست اندر جهان / ‏اندر او گاوى است تنها خوش دهان
entries.english: There is in the world a green island where a sweet-mouthed cow lives alone.

entries.farsi: جمله صحرا را چرد او تا به شب / ‏تا شود زفت و عظيم و منتجب
entries.english: She feeds on the whole field till nightfall, so that she grows stout and big and choice.

entries.farsi: شب ز انديشه كه فردا چه خورم / ‏گردد او چون تار مو الغر ز غم
entries.english: During the night she becomes thin as a hair from anxiety, because she thinks, “What shall I eat to-morrow?”

entries.farsi: چون بر آيد صبح گردد سبز دشت / ‏تا ميان رسته قصيل سبز و كشت
entries.english: At rise of dawn the field becomes green: the green blades and grain have grown up to middle.

entries.farsi: اندر افتد گاو با جوع البقر / تا به شب آن را چرد او سر به سر
entries.english: The cow falls to eating ravenously: till night she feeds on that entirely.

entries.farsi: باز زفت و فربه و لمتر شود / آن تنش از پيه و قوت پر شود
entries.english: Again she becomes stout and fat and bulky: her body is filled with fat and strength.

entries.farsi: باز شب اندر تب افتد از فزع / ‏تا شود الغر ز خوف منتجع
entries.english: Then again at night she by panic falls into a fever, so that from fear of seeking for fodder she becomes lean,

entries.farsi: كه چه خواهم خورد فردا وقت خور / سالها اين است كار آن بقر
entries.english: Thinking, “What shall I eat to-morrow at meal-time?” This is what that cow does for years.

entries.farsi: هيچ ننديشد كه چندين سال من / ‏مى‏خورم زين سبزه‏زار و زين چمن
entries.english: She never thinks, “All these years I have been eating from this meadow and this pasture;

entries.farsi: هيچ روزى كم نيامد روزى‏ام / ‏چيست اين ترس و غم و دل سوزى‏ام
entries.english: My food has never failed for a day: what, is this fear and anguish and heart-burning of mine?”

entries.farsi: باز چون شب مى‏شود آن گاو زفت / ‏مى‏شود الغر كه آوه رزق رفت
entries.english: No; when night falls that stout cow becomes lean again, thinking, “Alas, the provender is gone.”

entries.farsi: نفس آن گاو است و آن دشت اين جهان / ‏كاو همى الغر شود از خوف نان
entries.english: The cow is the carnal soul, and the field is this world, where she is made lean by fear for her daily bread,

entries.farsi: كه چه خواهم خورد مستقبل عجب / ‏لوت فردا از كجا سازم طلب
entries.english: Thinking, “I wonder what I shall eat in the future: whence shall I seek food for to-morrow?”

entries.farsi: سالها خوردى و كم نامد ز خور / ترك مستقبل كن و ماضى نگر
entries.english: You have eaten for years, and food has never failed: leave the future and look at the past.

entries.farsi: لوت و پوت خورده را هم ياد آر / منگر اندر غابر و كم باش زار
entries.english: Bring to mind the food and delicacies you have eaten: do not regard what is to come, and do not be miserable.

sections.title_fa: صيد كردن شير آن خر را و تشنه شدن شير از كوشش، رفت به چشمه تا آب خورد، تا باز آمدن شير جگر و دل و گرده را روباه خورده بود كه لطيف‏تر است، شير طلب كرد دل و جگر نيافت، از روباه پرسيد كه كو دل و جگر، روبه گفت اگر او را دل و جگر بودى آن چنان سياستى ديده بود آن روز و ِبه هزار حيله جان برده كى بر تو باز آمدى، لَوْ كُنَّا نَسْ مَعُ أَوْ نَعْقِلُ ما كُنَّا فِي أَصْ حابِ السَّ عِير

sections.title_en: How the lion made the ass his prey, and being thirsty after his exertions went to the spring to drink. Before his return the fox had eaten the liver together with the lungs, heart, and kidneys, which are the choicest parts. The lion looked for the heart and liver, and when he did not find them asked the fox where they were. The fox replied, “If he had possessed a heart and liver,

how should he have come back to you after receiving such a stern lesson on that day and saving his life by means of a thousand devices?” “If we had listened or considered with understanding we should not have been among the fellows of Hell-fire.”

entries.farsi: برد خر را روبهك تا پيش شير / پاره پاره كردش آن شير دلير
entries.english: The little fox brought the ass into the presence of the lion: the courageous lion tore him to pieces.

entries.farsi: تشنه شد از كوشش آن سلطان دد / رفت سوى چشمه تا آبى خورد
entries.english: The King of the Beasts was made thirsty by his exertions and went to the spring to drink some water.

entries.farsi: روبهك خورد آن جگر بند و دلش / ‏آن زمان چون فرصتى شد حاصلش
entries.english: Meanwhile the little fox, having got an opportunity, ate his liver, lungs, and heart.

entries.farsi: شير چون وا گشت از چشمه به خور / جست در خر دل نه دل بد نه جگر
entries.english: When the lion returned from the spring to eat, he looked in the ass to find the heart; there was neither heart nor liver.

entries.farsi: گفت روبه را جگر كو دل چه شد / كه نباشد جانور را زين دو بد
entries.english: He said to the fox, “Where is the liver? What has become of the heart? For no animal can do without these two.”

entries.farsi: آن قيامت ديده بود و رستخيز / و آن ز كوه افتادن و هول و گريز
entries.english: He replied, “If he had possessed a heart or liver, how should he have come here a second time?

entries.farsi: گفت گر بودى و را دل يا جگر / كى بدين جا آمدى بار دگر
entries.english: He had experienced that tremendous agony and turmoil, the scramble down the mountain, the terror, and the flight;

entries.farsi: گر جگر بودى و را يا دل بدى / ‏بار ديگر كى بر تو آمدى
entries.english: If he had had a liver or heart, how could he have come a second time into your presence?”

entries.farsi: چون نباشد نور دل دل نيست آن / ‏چون نباشد روح جز گل نيست آن
entries.english: When there is no light in the heart, it is no heart; when there is no spirit, it is nothing but earth.

entries.farsi: آن زجاجى كاو ندارد نور جان / ‏بول و قاروره دست قنديلش مخوان
entries.english: The glass that has no spiritual light is urine and the urine-phial: do not call it a lamp.

entries.farsi: نور مصباح است داد ذو الجلال / ‏صنعت خلق است آن شيشه و سفال
entries.english: The light in the lamp is the gift of the Almighty; the glass and earthenware are His creatures’ handiwork.

entries.farsi: آن جهود از ظرفها مشرك شده‏ست / ‏نور ديد آن مومن و مدرك شده‏ست
entries.english: Necessarily in respect of the vessels there is number, in respect of the flames there is nothing but unity.

entries.farsi: ال جرم در ظرف باشد اعتداد / در لهب‏ها نبود الا اتحاد
entries.english: When the light of six lamps is mingled together, there is no number and plurality in their light.

entries.farsi: نور شش قنديل چون آميختند / نيست اندر نورشان اعداد و چند
entries.english: The Jew has become a polytheist from the vessels; the true believer regarded the light and has become endowed with perception.

entries.farsi: چون نظر بر ظرف افتد روح را / پس دو بيند شيث را و نوح را
entries.english: When the sight falls upon the spirit’s vessel, it regards Seth and Noah as being two.

entries.farsi: چون كه آبش هست جو خود آن بود / آدمى آن است كاو را جان بود
entries.english: When there is water in the canal, only then is it a canal: the man is he that has the spirit.

entries.farsi: اين نه مردانند اينها صورتند / مرده‏ى نامند و كشته‏ى شهوتند
entries.english: These are not men, they are forms: they are dead with bread and killed by appetite.

sections.title_fa: حكايت آن راهب كه روز با چراغ مى‏گشت در ميان بازار از سر حالتى كه او را بود
sections.title_en: Story of the Christian ascetic who went about with a lamp in the daytime in the midst of the bazaar because of the ecstasy which he had in his heart.

entries.farsi: آن يكى با شمع بر مى‏گشت روز / گرد بازارى دلش پر عشق و سوز
entries.english: That person was going about in a bazaar in the daytime with a candle, his heart full of love and ardour.

entries.farsi: بو الفضولى گفت او را كاى فالن / ‏هين چه مى‏جويى به سوى هر دكان
entries.english: A busybody said to him, “Hey, O such-and-such, what are you seeking beside every shop?

entries.farsi: هين چه مى‏گردى تو جويان با چراغ / در ميان روز روشن چيست الغ‏
entries.english: Hey, why are you going about in search with a lamp in bright daylight? What is the joke?”

entries.farsi: گفت مى‏جويم به هر سو آدمى / كه بود حى از حيات آن دمى‏
entries.english: He replied, “I am searching everywhere for a man that is alive with the life inspired by that Breath.

entries.farsi: هست مردى گفت اين بازار پر / مردمانند آخر اى داناى حر
entries.english: Is there a man in existence?” “This bazaar,” said the other, “is full: surely they are men, O noble sage.”

entries.farsi: گفت خواهم مرد بر جاده‏ى دو ره / در ره خشم و به هنگام شره‏
entries.english: He answered, “I want a man on the two-way road— in the way of anger and at the time of desire.

entries.farsi: وقت خشم و وقت شهوت مرد كو / طالب مردى دوامن كو به كو
entries.english: Where is a man at the moment of anger and at the moment of appetite? In search of a man I am running from street to street.

entries.farsi: كو در اين دو حال مردى در جهان / تا فداى او كنم امروز جان‏
entries.english: Where in the world is a man on these two occasions, that I may devote my life to him to-day?”

entries.farsi: گفت نادر چيز مى‏جويى و ليك / غافل از حكم و قضايى بني تو نيك‏
entries.english: “You are seeking a rare thing,” said he; “but you take no heed of the ordinance and destiny. Consider well!

entries.farsi: ناظر فرعى ز اصلى بى‏خبر / فرع ماييم اصل احكام قدر
entries.english: You regard the branch; you are unaware of the root: we are the branch, the ordinances of the decree are the root.”

entries.farsi: چرخ گردان را قضا گمره كند / صد عطارد را قضا ابله كند
entries.english: The destiny causes the rolling sphere to lose its way; the Divine destiny makes a hundred Mercuries to be ignorant;

entries.farsi: تنگ گرداند جهان چاره را / آب گرداند حديد و خاره را
entries.english: It makes the world of contrivance to be straitened; it makes iron and hard rock to be water.

entries.farsi: اى قرارى داده ره را گام گام / خام خامى خام خامى خام خام‏
entries.english: O you who have resolved upon the way, step by step, you are the rawest of the raw, the rawest of the raw, the rawest of the raw.

entries.farsi: چون بديدى گردش سنگ آسيا / آب جو را هم ببني آخر بيا
entries.english: Since you have seen the revolution of the millstone, come now; see also the water of the river.

entries.farsi: خاك را ديدى بر آمد در هوا / در ميان خاك بنگر باد را
entries.english: You have seen the dust rise into the air: amidst the dust see the wind.

entries.farsi: ديگهاى فكر مى‏بينى به جوش / اندر آتش هم نظر مى‏كن به هوش‏
entries.english: You see the kettles of thought boiling: look with intelligence on the fire too.

entries.farsi: گفت حق ايوب را در مكرمت / من به هر موييت صبرى دادمت‏
entries.english: God said to Job, “I have graciously bestowed patience upon every hair of you.

entries.farsi: هين به صبر خود مكن چندين نظر / صبر ديدى صبر دادن را نگر
entries.english: Listen; do not pay so much regard to your patience: you have seen patience, look at My giving patience.”

entries.farsi: چند بينى گردش دوالب را / سر برون كن هم ببني تيز آب را
entries.english: How long will you behold the revolution of the water-wheel? Put forth your head and behold the rapid water.

entries.farsi: تو همى‏گويى كه مى‏بينم و ليك / ديد آن را بس عالمتهاست نيك‏
entries.english: You will say, “I am beholding it”; but there are many good signs of beholding it.

entries.farsi: گردش كف را چو ديدى مختصر / حيرتت بايد به دريا در نگر
entries.english: When you have taken a summary view of the circling movement of the foam, look upon the Sea if you want bewilderment.

entries.farsi: آن كه كف را ديد سر گويان بود / وان كه دريا ديد او حيران بود
entries.english: He that regards the foam tells of the mystery, while he that regards the Sea is bewildered.

entries.farsi: آن كه كف را ديد نيتها كند / وان كه دريا ديد دل دريا كند
entries.english: He that regards the foam forms intentions, while he that regards the Sea makes his heart one with the Sea.

entries.farsi: آن كه كفها ديد باشد در شمار / وان كه دريا ديد شد بى‏اختيار
entries.english: He that regards the foam-flakes is in reckoning, while he that regards the Sea is without volition.

entries.farsi: آن كه او كف ديد در گردش بود / وان كه دريا ديد او بى‏غش بود
entries.english: He that regards the foam is in movement, while he that regards the Sea is devoid of hypocrisy.

sections.title_fa: دعوت‏كردن مسلمان مغ را
sections.title_en: How a Moslem called a Magi to accept Islam.

entries.farsi: مر مغى را گفت مردى كاى فالن / ‏هين مسلمان شو بباش از مومنان
entries.english: A certain man said to a Magi, “O such-and-such, listen, become a Moslem, be one of the true believers!”

entries.farsi: گفت اگر خواهد خدا مومن شوم / ‏ور فزايد فضل هم موقن شوم
entries.english: He replied, “If God will, I shall become a true believer; and if He increase His grace, I shall become possessed of intuitive faith.”

entries.farsi: گفت مى‏خواهد خدا ايمان تو / تا رهد از دست دوزخ جان تو
entries.english: He said, “God wills your true belief, in order that your spirit may be delivered from the hand of Hell;

entries.farsi: ليك نفس نحس و آن شيطان زشت / ‏مى‏كشندت سوى كفران و كنشت
entries.english: But your ill-omened carnal soul and the wicked Devil are dragging you towards infidelity and the fire-temple.”

entries.farsi: يار آن تانم بدن كاو غالب است / ‏آن طرف افتم كه غالب جاذب است
entries.english: He replied, “O reasonable man, since they are predominant I shall be on the side of the stronger.

entries.farsi: گفت اى منصف چو ايشان غالب‏اند / يار او باشم كه باشد زورمند
entries.english: I can side with him who is predominant: I fall in the direction to which the predominant one is pulling.

entries.farsi: چون خدا مى‏خواست از من صدق زفت / ‏خواست او چه سود چون پيشش نرفت
entries.english: Since God desired a firm belief of me, what is the use of His desire when He does not succeed?

entries.farsi: نفس و شيطان خواست خود را پيش برد / و آن عنايت قهر گشت و خرد و مرد
entries.english: The carnal soul and the Devil have carried their will to success, while that act of favour has been defeated and pulverised.

entries.farsi: تو يكى قصر و سرايى ساختى / ‏اندر او صد نقش خوش افراختى
entries.english: It is as if you had built a palace and pavilion and erected therein a hundred beautiful designs,

entries.farsi: خواستى مسجد بود آن جاى خير / ديگرى آمد مر آن را ساخت دير
entries.english: And desired that that goodly place should be a mosque— and someone else had come and made it a Christian monastery;

entries.farsi: يا تو بافيدى يكى كرباس تا / خوش بسازى بهر پوشيدن قبا
entries.english: Or you had woven a piece of linen cloth, in order deftly to make it a coat for someone to wear,

entries.farsi: تو قبا مى‏خواستى خصم از نبرد / رغم تو كرباس را شلوار كرد
entries.english: And you wanted a coat, a rival, from hostility, made the linen stuff into a pair of trousers in spite of you.

entries.farsi: چاره‏ى كرباس چه بود جان من / ‏جز زبون راى آن غالب شدن
entries.english: What resource has the linen, my dear friend, but to submit to the purpose of the one who is predominant?

entries.farsi: او زبون شد جرم اين كرباس چيست / ‏آن كه او مغلوب غالب نيست كيست
entries.english: He is reduced to submission, what is the crime of this linen? Who is he that is not dominated by the predominant?

entries.farsi: چون كسى بى‏خواست او بر وى براند / خار بن در ملك و خانه‏ى او نشاند
entries.english: When someone has forced his way in against his will and planted a thorn bush in his property and house,

entries.farsi: صاحب خانه بدين خوارى بود / كه چنين بر وى خالقت مى‏رود
entries.english: The master of the house is humiliated because such shabbiness is inflicted on him.

entries.farsi: هم خلق گردم من ار تازه و نوم / ‏چون كه يار اين چنين خوارى شوم
entries.english: I too, though I am fresh and new, would become shabby through being associated with such a contemptible person.

entries.farsi: من اگر ننگ مغان يا كافرم / ‏آن نيم كه بر خدا اين ظن برم
entries.english: Since the will of the carnal soul is besought for help, it is mockery that whatever God wills shall come to pass.

entries.farsi: چون كه خواه نفس آمد مستعان / ‏تسخر آمد ايش شاء اللهَّ كان
entries.english: If I am a disgrace to the Magis or am an infidel, I am not such as to think this of God,

entries.farsi: كه كسى ناخواه او و رغم او / گردد اندر ملكت او حكم جو
entries.english: That anyone should seek to exercise authority in His kingdom against His will and in spite of Him,

entries.farsi: ملكت او را فرو گيرد چنين / ‏كه نيارد دم زدن دم آفرين
entries.english: And thus should occupy His kingdom, so that the Creator of the breath dare not breathe a word,

entries.farsi: دفع او مى‏خواهد و مى‏بايدش / ‏ديو هر دم غصه مى‏افزايدش
entries.english: He wishes to repel him and must do so; the Devil at every moment is increasing His anxiety.

entries.farsi: بنده‏ى اين ديو مى‏بايد شدن / ‏چون كه غالب اوست در هر انجمن
entries.english: I must worship the Devil, inasmuch as he is predominant in every assembly,

entries.farsi: تا مبادا كين كشد شيطان ز من / ‏پس چه دستم گيرد آن جا ذو المنن
entries.english: Lest the Devil take vengeance on me— and then in that case how can the Gracious lend me a hand?

entries.farsi: آن كه او خواهد مراد او شود / از كه كار من دگر نيكو شود
entries.english: That which he wills, his desire is fulfilled: by whom shall my affairs be restored to prosperity?

sections.title_fa: ‏مثل شيطان بر در رحمان
sections.title_en: Parable of the Devil at the door of the Merciful God.

entries.farsi: حاش هلل ايش شاء اللهَّ كان / ‏حاكم آمد در مكان و ال مكان
entries.english: God forbid! Whatever God wills shall come to pass. He is the Ruler over the worlds of space and non-spatiality.

entries.farsi: هيچ كس در ملك او بى‏امر او / در نيفزايد سر يك تاى مو
entries.english: Without His command no one in His kingdom shall add the tip of a single hair.

entries.farsi: ملك ملك اوست فرمان آن او / كمترين سگ بر در آن شيطان او
entries.english: The kingdom is His kingdom, the command is His: that Devil of His is the meanest dog at His door.

entries.farsi: تركمان را گر سگى باشد به در / بر درش بنهاده باشد رو و سر
entries.english: If a Turcoman’s dog is lying at the door, with his face and head resting on the threshold,

entries.farsi: كودكان خانه دمش مى‏كشند / باشد اندر دست طفالن خوارمند
entries.english: If the children of the house keep pulling his tail, he will be humble in the children’s hands.

entries.farsi: باز اگر بيگانه‏اى معبر كند / حمله بر وى همچو شير نر كند
entries.english: If, however, a stranger passes by, he will rush at him like a fierce lion;

entries.farsi: كه أَشِدَّاءُ عَلَى الكفار شد / با ولى گل با عدو چون خار شد
entries.english: For he is hard on the unbelievers: to a friend he is the rose, to an enemy as the thorn.

entries.farsi: ز آب تتماجى كه دادش تركمان / آن چنان وافى شده‏ست و پاسبان
entries.english: He has become so faithful and vigilant on account of the tutmáj broth that the Turcoman has given him.

entries.farsi: پس سگ شيطان كه حق هستش كند / اندر او صد فكرت و حيلت تند
entries.english: The dog, then, namely the Devil, whom God causes to exist and in whom He creates a hundred thoughts and cunning plans,

entries.farsi: آب تتماج است آب روى عام / ‏كه سگ شيطان از آن يابد طعام
entries.english: And whom He feeds with honours, so that he takes away the honour of the virtuous and the wicked—

entries.farsi: آب روها را غذاى او كند / تا برد او آب روى نيك و بد
entries.english: The honour of the populace is the tutmáj broth by which the Devil-dog is fed—

entries.farsi: بر در خر گاه قدرت جان او / چون نباشد حكم را قربان بگو
entries.english: Tell me, how should not his soul be devoted to the decree at the door of the tent of Omnipotence?

entries.farsi: گله گله از مريد و از مريد / چون سگ باسط ذراعى بالوصيد
entries.english: Troop on troop of obedient and rebellious, like the dog spreading his fore-paws on the threshold,

entries.farsi: اى سگ ديو امتحان مى‏كن كه تا / چون در اين ره مى‏نهند اين خلق پا
entries.english: Are stationed like dogs at the door of the Cave of the Godhead, seeking the command with every particle, and with every nerve agog,

entries.farsi: بر در كهف الوهيت چو سگ / ‏ذره ذره امر جو بر جسته رگ
entries.english: ‘O Devil-dog, inflict tribulation in order that how these creatures set foot on this Way.

entries.farsi: حمله مى‏كن منع مى‏كن مى‏نگر / تا كه باشد ماده اندر صدق و نر
entries.english: Continually rush, prevent, and look to see who is female (weak) in respect of sincerity, and who is male (strong).’

entries.farsi: پس اعوذ از بهر چه باشد چو سگ / ‏گشته باشد از ترفع تيز تگ
entries.english: For what purpose, then, is, ‘I take refuge’ when the Dog in his arrogance has run swiftly?

entries.farsi: اين اعوذ آن است كاى ترك خطا / بانگ بر زن بر سگت ره برگشا
entries.english: This, ‘I take refuge,’ is, ‘O Turcoman of Khitá, call your dog off and leave the way clear,

entries.farsi: تا بيايم بر در خرگاه تو / حاجتى خواهم ز جود و جاه تو
entries.english: That I may come to the door of your tent and beg what I need from your bounty and high estate.’

entries.farsi: چون كه ترك از سطوت سگ عاجز است / ‏اين اعوذ و اين فغان ناجايز است
entries.english: When the Turcoman is incapable of the dog’s fury, this, ‘I take refuge,’ and this cry of distress are improper,

entries.farsi: ترك هم گويد اعوذ از سگ كه من / ‏هم ز سگ درمانده‏ام اندر وطن
entries.english: The Turcoman too will say, ‘I take refuge from the dog; for I too am helpless against the dog in my home.

entries.farsi: تو نمى‏يارى بر اين در آمدن / ‏من نمى‏آرم ز در بيرون شدن
entries.english: You cannot come to this door, nor can I go forth from the door.’

entries.farsi: خاك اكنون بر سر ترك و قنق / ‏كه يكى سگ هر دو را بندد عنق
entries.english: Now dust is on the head of the Turcoman and the stranger-guest, since one dog binds the necks of them both!

entries.farsi: حاش هلل ترك بانگى بر زند / سگ چه باشد شير نر خون قى كند
entries.english: God forbid! If the Turcoman utter a shout, what of the dog? A fierce lion would vomit blood.”

entries.farsi: اى كه خود را شير يزدان خوانده‏اى / ‏سالها شد با سگى درمانده‏اى
entries.english: O you who have called yourself “the Lion of God,” for years you have been powerless against a dog.

entries.farsi: چون كند اين سگ براى تو شكار / چون شكار سگ شده ستى آشكار
entries.english: How should this dog hunt on your behalf when you have manifestly become a prey to the dog?

sections.title_fa: جواب گفتن مومن سنى كافر جبرى را و در اثبات اختيار بنده دليل گفتن، سنت راهى باشد كوفته‏ى اقدام انبيا عليهم السلام بر يمين آن راه بيابان جبر كه خود را اختيار نبيند و امر و نهى را منكر شود و تاويل كند، و از منكر شدن امر و نهى الزم آيد انكار بهشت كه بهشت جزاى مطيعان امر است و دوزخ جزاى مخالفان امر، و ديگر نگويم به چه انجامد كه العاقل يكفيه الاشاره، و بر يسار آن راه بيابان قدر است كه قدرت خالق را مغلوب قدرت خلق داند و از آن فسادها زايد كه آن مغ جبرى بر
شمرد
sections.title_en: The reply of the Sunni believer to the Necessitarian infidel, and the proof by which he established the power of choice possessed by servant of God. The Sunna is a road trodden by the feet of the

prophets, on whom is peace. On the right hand of that road lies the desert of Necessity (jabr), where the Necessitarian regards himself as having no power of choice and denies the command and prohibition and employs interpretation (ta’wíl); and from the command and prohibition being denied there necessarily follows the denial of Paradise, since Paradise is the reward of those

who obey the command, while Hell is the reward of those who disobey it. I will not state to what else it ultimately leads: an indication is enough for the wise. And on the left hand of that road lies the desert of Freewill (qadar), where he regards the power of the Creator as overcome by the power of the creatures; and thence arise the corruptions
which have been enumerated by the Magi who was a Necessitarian.

entries.farsi: گفت مومن بشنو اى جبرى خطاب / ‏آن خود گفتى نك آوردم جواب
entries.english: The true believer replied, “O Necessitarian, hear the words addressed; you have said your say: lo, I bring the answer.

entries.farsi: بازى خود ديدى اى شطرنج باز / بازى خصمت ببين پهن و دراز
entries.english: You have seen your own game, O chess-player: see your adversary’s game in all its breadth and length.

entries.farsi: نامه‏ى عذر خودت بر خواندى / نكته گفتى جبريانه در قضا
entries.english: You have read your letter of apology: read the Sunní’s letter. Why have you remained?

entries.farsi: سر آن بشنو ز من در ماجرا / اختيارى هست ما را بى‏گمان
entries.english: You have discoursed in Necessitarian fashion on the destiny: hear from me the mystery thereof in debate.

entries.farsi: ‏حس را منكر نتانى شد عيان / سنگ را هرگز نگويد كس بيا
entries.english: Beyond doubt we possess a certain power of choice: you cannot deny the plain evidence of the sense.

entries.farsi: از كلوخى كس كجا جويد وفا / آدمى را كس نگويد هين بپر
entries.english: One never says ‘Come’ to a stone: how should any one request a brickbat to keep faith?

entries.farsi: يا بيا اى كور تو در من نگر / گفت يزدان ما على الاعمى حرج
entries.english: One never says to a human being, ‘Hey, fly!’ or ‘Come, O blind man, and look at me!’

entries.farsi: ‏كى نهد بر كس حرج رب الفرج / كس نگويد سنگ را دير آمدى
entries.english: God has said, ‘there is nothing intolerable laid upon the blind’: how should the Lord who bestows relief lay upon any one what is intolerable?

entries.farsi: ‏يا كه چو با تو چرا بر من زدى / اين چنين واجستها مجبور را
entries.english: Nobody says to a stone, ‘You have come late or, ‘Why did you strike a blow at me, O stick?’

entries.farsi: كس بگويد يا زند معذور را / امر و نهى و خشم و تشريف و عتاب
entries.english: Will any one address demands like these to a person who is compelled, or strike a person who is excused?

entries.farsi: ‏نيست جز مختار را اى پاك جيب / اختيارى هست در ظلم و ستم
entries.english: Command and prohibition and anger and conferment of honour and rebuke concern him who possesses the power of choice, O pure bosomed one.

entries.farsi: ‏من از اين شيطان و نفس اين خواستم / اختيار اندر درونت ساكن است
entries.english: There is a power of choice in regard to injustice and wrong-doing: this I meant by this Devil and carnal soul.

entries.farsi: ‏تا نديد او يوسفى كف را نخست / اختيار و داعيه در نفس بود
entries.english: The power of choice resides in your inward part; it does not wound its hand till it sees a Joseph.

entries.farsi: روش ديد آن گه پر و بالى گشود / سگ بخفته اختيارش گشته گم
entries.english: The power of choice and the instinct were in the soul: it beheld his face, and then it spread wings and pinions.

entries.farsi: ‏چون شكنبه ديد جنبانيد دم / اسب هم حو حو كند چون ديد جو
entries.english: When the dog is asleep its power of choice is lost, when it sees the tripe it wags its tail.

entries.farsi: چون بجنبد گوشت گربه كرد مو / ديدن آمد جنبش آن اختيار
entries.english: A horse, too, whinnies when he sees barley, and when the meat is moved, the cat meows.

entries.farsi: همچو نفخى ز آتش انگيزد شرار / پس بجنبد اختيارت چون بليس
entries.english: Sight is the means of moving the power of choice, just as blowing raises sparks from the fire.

entries.farsi: ‏شد دالله آردت پيغام ويس / چون كه مطلوبى بر اين كس عرضه كرد
entries.english: Therefore your power of choice is moved when Iblís becomes a go-between (dallála) and brings a message from Wís.

entries.farsi: اختيار خفته بگشايد نورد / و آن فرشته خيرها بر رغم ديو
entries.english: When he presents an object of desire to this person, the sleeping power unrolls itself;

entries.farsi: عرضه دارد مى‏كند در دل غريو / تا بجنبد اختيار خير تو
entries.english: And, in despite of the Devil, the Angel presents good objects and makes an outcry in your heart,

entries.farsi: ز انكه پيش از عرضه خفته‏ست اين دو خو / پس فرشته و ديو گشته عرضه دار
entries.english: In order that your power to choose the good may be moved; for, before presentation, these two dispositions are asleep

entries.farsi: بهر تحريك عروق اختيار / مى‏شود ز الهامها و وسوسه
entries.english: So the Angel and the Devil have become presenters for the purpose of setting the power of choice in motion.

entries.farsi: ‏اختيار خير و شرت ده كسه / وقت تحليل نماز اى با نمك
entries.english: Your power to choose good or evil is increased tenfold by inspirations and suggestions.

entries.farsi: ‏ز آن سالم آورد بايد بر ملك / كه ز الهام و دعاى خوبتان
entries.english: Hence, when your ritual prayer is finished, O excellent man, it behooves you to offer a salutation to the Angels,

entries.farsi: ‏اختيار اين نمازم شد روان / باز از بعد گنه لعنت كنى
entries.english: Saying, ‘Through your goodly inspiration and incitement, my power to choose this ritual prayer was set going.’

entries.farsi: ‏بر بليس ايرا كز اويى منحنى / اين دو ضد عرضه كنندت در سرار
entries.english: Again, after sin you will curse Iblís, because through him you are inclined.

entries.farsi: در حجاب غيب آمد عرضه دار / چون كه پرده‏ى غيب بر خيزد ز پيش
entries.english: These two adversaries are making offers to you in secret and presenting in the curtain over the Unseen.

entries.farsi: ‏تو ببينى روى دالالن خويش / وز سخنشان واشناسى بى‏گزند
entries.english: When the curtain over the Unseen is raised from before you, you will behold the faces of your brokers,

entries.farsi: كان سخن گويان نهان اينها بدند / ديو گويد اى اسير طبع و تن
entries.english: And from their words will recognise without trouble that these were they who spoke invisibly.

entries.farsi: ‏عرضه مى‏كردم نكردم زور من / و آن فرشته گويدت من گفتمت
entries.english: The Devil will say, ‘O you who art a captive to your nature and body, I was (only) presenting to you: I did not force.’

entries.farsi: ‏كه از اين شادى فزون گردد غمت / آن فالن روزت نگفتم من چنان
entries.english: And the Angel will say, ‘I told you that your sorrow would be increased in consequence of this joy.

entries.farsi: ‏كه از آن سوى است ره سوى جنان / ما محب جان و روح افزاى تو
entries.english: Did not I tell you on such and such a day that the way to Paradise is in that direction?

entries.farsi: ساجدان مخلص باباى تو / اين زمانت خدمتى هم مى‏كنيم
entries.english: We are lovers of your soul and fosterers of your spirit and sincere worshippers of your Father?

entries.farsi: ‏سوى مخدومى صاليت مى‏زنيم / آن گره بابات را بوده عدى
entries.english: At this time also we are serving you and inviting you towards sovereignty?

entries.farsi: در خطاب اسْ جُدُوا كرده ابا / آن گرفتى آن ما انداختى
entries.english: That party was your Father’s enemies who refused to obey the command, Worship Adam?

entries.farsi: ‏حق خدمتهاى ما نشناختى / اين زمان ما را و ايشان را عيان
entries.english: You did accept that, you did reject ours: you did not acknowledge the debt due for our services.

entries.farsi: ‏در نگر بشناس از لحن و بيان / نيم شب چون بشنوى رازى ز دوست
entries.english: Now look on us and them in clear view, and recognise by voice and speech.’

entries.farsi: ‏چون سخن گويد سحر دانى كه اوست / ور دو كس در شب خبر آرد ترا
entries.english: If you hear a secret from a friend at midnight, you will know that it was he when he speaks at dawn;

entries.farsi: روز از گفتن شناسى هر دو را / بانگ شير و بانگ سگ در شب رسيد
entries.english: And if two persons bring news to you in the night, you will recognise both of them in the daytime by their speaking.

entries.farsi: صورت هر دو ز تاريكى نديد / روز شد چون باز در بانگ آمدند
entries.english: During the night the sound of a lion and the sound of a dog have come and he has not seen their forms on account of the darkness,

entries.farsi: پس شناسدشان ز بانگ آن هوشمند / مخلص اين كه ديو و روح عرضه دار
entries.english: When day breaks and they begin to make sound again, the intelligent will know them by the sound.

entries.farsi: هر دو هستند از تتمه‏ى اختيار / اختيارى هست در ما ناپديد
entries.english: The upshot is this that both the Devil and the Spirit who present exist for the purpose of completing the power of choice.

entries.farsi: چون دو مطلب ديد آيد در مزيد / اوستادان كودكان را مى‏زنند
entries.english: There is an invisible power of choice within us; when it sees two objects of desire it waxes strong.

entries.farsi: آن ادب سنگ سيه را كى كنند / هيچ گويى سنگ را فردا بيا
entries.english: Teachers beat children: how should they inflict that correction upon a black stone?

entries.farsi: هيچ با سنگى عتابى كس كند / در خرد جبر از قدر رسواتر است
entries.english: Do you ever say to a stone, ‘Come to-morrow; and if you don’t come, I will give your bad behaviour the punishment it deserves’?

entries.farsi: ور نيايى من دهم بد را سزا / هيچ عاقل مر كلوخى را زند
entries.english: Does any reasonable man strike a brickbat? Does anyone reprove a stone?

entries.farsi: ‏ز انكه جبرى حس خود را منكر است / منكر حس نيست آن مرد قدر
entries.english: In reason, Necessitarianism (jabr) is more shameful than the doctrine of Free-will (qadar), because the Necessitarian is denying his own sense.

entries.farsi: فعل حق حسى نباشد اى پسر / منكر فعل خداوند جليل
entries.english: The man who holds the doctrine of Free-will does not deny his sense: ‘The action of God is not mediated by the senses, O son.’

entries.farsi: ‏هست در انكار مدلول دليل / آن بگويد دود هست و نار نى
entries.english: He who denies the action of the Almighty Lord is denying Him who is indicated by the indication.

entries.farsi: نيست مى‏گويد پى انكار را / جامه‏اش سوزد بگويد نار نيست
entries.english: That one says, ‘There is smoke, but no fire; there is candle-light without any resplendent candle’;

entries.farsi: ‏نور شمعى بى‏ز شمعى روشنى / وين همى‏بيند معين نار را
entries.english: And this one sees the fire plainly, for the sake of denial he says it does not exist.

entries.farsi: ‏جامه‏اش دوزد بگويد تار نيست / پس تفسطط آمد اين دعوى جبر
entries.english: It burns his raiment, he says, ‘There is no fire’; it stitches his raiment, he says, ‘There is no thread.’

entries.farsi: ال جرم بدتر بود زين رو ز گبر / گبر گويد هست عالم نيست رب
entries.english: Hence this doctrine of Necessity is Skepticism: consequently he, from this point of view, is worse than the infidel.

entries.farsi: ‏يا ربى گويد كه نبود مستحب / اين همى‏گويد جهان خود نيست هيچ
entries.english: The infidel says, ‘The world exists, there is no Lord’: he says that ‘O my Lord!’ is not to be approved.

entries.farsi: امر و نهى اين بيار و آن ميار / او همى‏گويد كه امر و نهى الست
entries.english: This one says, ‘The world is really nothing’: the Sophist is in a tangle of error.

entries.farsi: ‏هست سوفسطايى اندر پيچ پيچ / جمله‏ى عالم مقر در اختيار
entries.english: The whole world acknowledges the power of choice: their commanding and forbidding —‘Bring this and do not bring that!’

entries.farsi: ‏اختيارى نيست اين جمله خطاست / حس را حيوان مقر است اى رفيق
entries.english: He says that commanding and forbidding are naught and that there is no power of choice. All this is erroneous.

entries.farsi: ‏ليك ادراك دليل آمد دقيق / ز انكه محسوس است ما را اختيار
entries.english: Animals acknowledge the reality of the inward sense, O comrade, but it is a subtle matter to apprehend the proof.

entries.farsi: خوب مى‏آيد بر او تكليف كار
entries.english: Inasmuch as our power of choice is perceived by the sense, responsibility for actions may well be laid upon it.

sections.title_fa: درك وجدانى چون اختيار و اضطرار و خشم و اصطبار و سيرى و ناهار به جاى حس است كه زرد از سرخ بداند و فرق كند و خرد از بزرگ و تلخ از شيرين و مشك از سرگين و درشت از نرم به حس مس و گرم از سرد و سوزان از شير گرم و تر از خشك و مس ديوار از مس درخت، پس منكر وجدانى منكر حس باشد و زياده كه وجدانى از حس ظاهر تر است زيرا حس را توان بستن و منع
‏كردن از احساس و بستن راه و مدخل وجدانيات را ممكن نيست، و العاقل يكفيه الاشاره
sections.title_en: The inward consciousness of having the power to choose or of acting under compulsion, of anger or self-restraint, of repletion or hunger, corresponds to the senses that know and distinguish yellow from red and small from great and bitter from sweet and musk from shit and hard from soft—by the sense of touch—and hot from cold and burning from lukewarm and wet from dry and contact with a wall from contact with a tree.

Therefore he who denies inward consciousness denies the senses, and more, inward consciousness is more evident than the senses, inasmuch as one can bind the senses and prevent them from functioning, while it is impossible to bar the way to the experiences of inward consciousness and stop their entrance. And an indication is enough for the wise.

entries.farsi: درك وجدانى به جاى حس بود / هر دو در يك جدول اى عم مى‏رود
entries.english: Inward consciousness corresponds to sensation: both run in the same channel, O uncle.

entries.farsi: نغز مى‏آيد بر او كن يا مكن / ‏امر و نهى و ماجراها و سخن
entries.english: ‘Do’ or ‘don’t,’ command and prohibition, discussions and talk are suitable to it.

entries.farsi: اين كه فردا اين كنم يا آن كنم / ‏اين دليل اختيار است اى صنم
entries.english: ‘To-morrow I will do this or I will do that,’ is a proof of the power to choose, O worshipful one;

entries.farsi: و آن پشيمانى كه خوردى ز آن بدى / ‏ز اختيار خويش گشتى مهتدى
entries.english: And the penitence which you have felt for an evil deed, you have been led through your power of choice.

entries.farsi: جمله قرآن امر و نهى است و وعيد / امر كردن سنگ مرمر را كه ديد
entries.english: The entire Qur’án consists of commands and prohibitions and threats: who saw commands given to a marble rock?

entries.farsi: هيچ دانا هيچ عاقل اين كند / با كلوخ و سنگ خشم و كين كند
entries.english: Does any wise man, does any reasonable man, do this? Does he show anger and enmity to brickbats and stones?—

entries.farsi: كه بگفتم كه چنين كن يا چنان / ‏چون نكرديد اى موات و عاجزان
entries.english: Saying, ‘I told you to do thus or thus: why have you not done it, O dead and helpless ones?’

entries.farsi: عقل كى حكمى كند بر چوب و سنگ / ‏عقل كى چنگى زند بر نقش چنگ
entries.english: How should reason exercise any authority over wood and stone? How should reason lay hold of the painted figure of a cripple?

entries.farsi: كاى غالم بسته دست اشكسته پا / خالقى كه اختر و گردون كند
entries.english: Saying, ‘O slave with palsied hands and broken legs, take up the lance and come to battle’?

entries.farsi: امر و نهى جاهالنه چون كند / احتمال عجز از حق راندى
entries.english: How should the Creator who is the Maker of stars and sky make commands and prohibitions like those of an ignorant person?

entries.farsi: ‏جاهل و گيج و سفيهش خواندى / عجز نبود از قدر ور خود شود
entries.english: You have removed from God the possibility of impotence, you have called Him ignorant and stupid and foolish.

entries.farsi: جاهلى از عاجزى بدتر بود / ترك مى‏گويد قنق را از كرم
entries.english: Impotence does not follow from the doctrine of Free-will; and even if it do, ignorance is worse than impotence.

entries.farsi: ‏بى‏سگ و بى‏دلق آ سوى درم / وز فالن سوى اندر آ هين با ادب
entries.english: The Turcoman says graciously to the stranger-guest, ‘Come to my door without a dog and without a tattered cloak,

entries.farsi: ‏تا سگم بندد ز تو دندان و لب / تو بعكس آن كنى بر در روى
entries.english: And listen, come in respectfully from such and such a quarter, in order that my dog may keep his teeth and mouth closed and refrain from biting you.’

entries.farsi: ‏ال جرم از زخم سگ خسته شوى / آن چنان رو كه غالمان رفته‏اند
entries.english: You do the reverse of that and advance to the door: necessarily you are wounded by the violence of the dog.

entries.farsi: تا سگش گردد حليم و مهرمند / تو سگى با خود برى يا روبهى
entries.english: You must advance in the same pounder in which slaves have advanced, so that his dog may become gentle and affectionate.

entries.farsi: خشم چون مى‏آيدت بر جرم دار / چون همى‏خايى تو دندان بر عدو
entries.english: You take a dog or a fox with you, a dog will rage from the bottom of every tent.

entries.farsi: ‏سگ بشورد از بن هر خر گهى / غير حق را گر نباشد اختيار
entries.english: If none but God has the power of choice, why do you become angry with one who has committed an offence?

entries.farsi: بر تو افتد سخت مجروحت كند / هيچ خشمى آيدت بر چوب سقف
entries.english: Why do you gnash your teeth at a foe? Why do you regard the sin and offence as from him?

entries.farsi: چون همى‏بينى گناه و جرم از او / گر ز سقف خانه چوبى بشكند
entries.english: If a piece of timber break off from your house-roof and fall upon you and wound you severely,

entries.farsi: ‏هيچ اندر كين او باشى تو وقف / كه چرا بر من زد و دستم شكست
entries.english: Will you feel any anger against the timber of the roof? Will you ever devote yourself to taking vengeance upon it?

entries.farsi: ‏او عدو و خصم جان من بده‏ست / كودكان خرد را چون مى‏زنى
entries.english: ‘Why did it hit me and fracture my hand? It has been my mortal foe and enemy’?

entries.farsi: ‏چون بزرگان را منزه مى‏كنى / آن كه دزدد مال تو گويى بگير
entries.english: Why do you beat little children, since you make out that adults are exempt from blame?

entries.farsi: دست و پايش را ببر سازش اسير / وان كه قصد عورت تو مى‏كند
entries.english: A man who steals your property, you say, ‘Arrest him, cut off his hand and foot, make him a captive’;

entries.farsi: صد هزاران خشم از تو مى‏دمد / گر بيايد سيل و رخت تو برد
entries.english: And a man who visits your wife, a hundred thousand angers shoot up from you.

entries.farsi: هيچ با سيل آورد كينى خرد / ور بيامد باد و دستارت ربود
entries.english: If a flood come and sweep away your household goods, will your reason bear any enmity towards the flood?

entries.farsi: كى ترا با باد دل خشمى نمود / خشم در تو شد بيان اختيار
entries.english: And if the wind came and carried off your turban, when did your heart show any anger against the wind?

entries.farsi: تا نگويى جبريانه اعتذار / گر شتربان اشترى را مى‏زند
entries.english: The anger within you is a clear demonstration of a power of choice, so that you must not excuse yourself after the fashion of Necessitarians.

entries.farsi: آن شتر قصد زننده مى‏كند / خشم اشتر نيست با آن چوب او
entries.english: If a camel-driver goes on striking a camel, the camel will attack the striker.

entries.farsi: پس ز مختارى شتر برده‏ست بو / همچنين سگ گر بر او سنگى زنى
entries.english: The camel’s anger is not against his stick: therefore the camel has got some notion of the power of choice.

entries.farsi: ‏بر تو آرد حمله گردد منثنى / سنگ را گر گيرد از خشم تو است
entries.english: Similarly a dog, if you throw a stone at him, will rush at you and become contorted.

entries.farsi: اين مگو اى عقل انسان شرم دار / روشن است اين ليك از طمع سحور
