entries.english: There was an Amír of merry heart, exceedingly fond of wine: the refuge of every drunkard and every resourceless person.

entries.farsi: مشفقى مسكين نوازى عادلى / ‏جوهرى زر بخششى دريا دلى
entries.english: A compassionate man, kind to the poor and just; a jewel, gold-lavishing, ocean-hearted;

entries.farsi: شاه مردان و امير المؤمنين / ‏راه‏بان و راز دان و دوست بين
entries.english: A king of men and commander of the Faithful; a keeper of the Way and a knower of secrets and a discerner of friends

entries.farsi: دور عيسى بود و ايام مسيح / ‏خلق دل دار و كم آزار و مليح
entries.english: It was the epoch of Jesus and the days of the Messiah: he was beloved of the people and unoppressive and agreeable.

entries.farsi: آمدش مهمان به ناگاهان شبى / ‏هر اميرى جنس او خوش مذهبى
entries.english: Suddenly one night, another Amír, a person of good principles congenial to him, came seeking his hospitality.

entries.farsi: باده مى بايستشان در نظم حال / ‏باده بود آن وقت مأذون و حالل
entries.english: They wanted wine in order to enjoy themselves: at that period wine was permissible and lawful;

entries.farsi: باده‏شان كم بود و گفتا اى غالم / ‏رو سبو پر كن به ما آور مدام
entries.english: They had no wine, so he said to his slave, “Go, fill the jug and fetch us wine

entries.farsi: از فالن راهب كه دارد خمر خاص / ‏تا ز خاص و عام يابد جان خالص
entries.english: From such-and-such a Christian ascetic who has choice wine, that the soul may win release from high and low.”

entries.farsi: اندر آن مى مايه‏ى پنهانى است / ‏آن چنانك اندر عبا سلطانى است
entries.english: One draught from the Christian ascetic’s cup has the same effect as thousands of wine-jars and wine-cellars.

entries.farsi: جرعه‏اى ز آن جام راهب آن كند / كه هزاران جره و خمدان كند
entries.english: In that wine there is a hidden substance, even as sovereignty is hidden in the dervish-cloak.

entries.farsi: تو به دلق پاره پاره كم نگر / كه سيه كردند از بيرون زر
entries.english: Do not regard the tattered cloak, for they have put black on the outside of the gold.

entries.farsi: از براى چشم بد مردود شد / وز برون آن لعل دود آلود شد
entries.english: On account of the evil eye the dervish becomes immoral, and that ruby is tarnished with smoke on the outside.

entries.farsi: گنج و گوهر كى ميان خانه‏هاست / ‏گنجها پيوسته در ويرانه‏هاست
entries.english: When are treasures and jewels in the rooms of a house? Treasures are always in ruins.

entries.farsi: گنج آدم چون به ويران بد دفين / ‏گشت طينش چشم بند آن لعين
entries.english: Since Adam’s treasure was buried in a ruin, his clay became a bandage over the eye of the accursed.

entries.farsi: او نظر مى‏كرد در طين سست سست / ‏جان همى‏گفتش كه طينم سد تست
entries.english: He was regarding the clay with the utmost contempt, the spirit was saying, “My clay is a barrier to you.”

entries.farsi: دو سبو بستد غالم و خوش دويد / در زمان در دير رهبانان رسيد
entries.english: The slave took two jugs and ran with goodwill: immediately he arrived at the monastery of the Christian monks.

entries.farsi: زر بداد و باده‏ى چون زر خريد / سنگ داد و در عوض گوهر خريد
entries.english: He paid gold and purchased wine like gold: he gave stones and bought jewels in exchange.

entries.farsi: باده‏اى كان بر سر شاهان جهد / تاج زر بر تارك ساقى نهد
entries.english: A wine that would fly to the head of kings and put a golden tiara on the crown of the cupbearer’s head.

entries.farsi: استخوانها رفته جمله جان شده / ‏تخت و تخته آن زمان يكسان شده
entries.english: By it troubles and commotions are aroused, slaves and emperors are mingled together;

entries.farsi: فتنه‏ها و شورها انگيخته / ‏بندگان و خسروان آميخته
entries.english: Bones vanish and become spirit entirely; throne and bench at that moment become alike.

entries.farsi: وقت هشيارى چو آب و روغنند / وقت مستى همچو جان اندر تنند
entries.english: They, when sober, are as water and oil; when intoxicated, they are as the spirit in the body.

entries.farsi: چون هريسه گشته آن جا فرق نيست / ‏نيست فرقى كاندر آن جا غرق نيست
entries.english: They become like a stew (harisa): no difference exists there: there is no difference that is not submerged there.

entries.farsi: اين چنين باده همى‏برد آن غالم / ‏سوى قصر آن امير نيك نام
entries.english: The slave was carrying a wine of this sort to the palace of the Amír of good renown,

entries.farsi: پيشش آمد زاهدى غم ديده‏اى / ‏خشك مغزى در بال پيچيده‏اى
entries.english: An ascetic met him, one who had suffered anguish, whose brain was dry, and who was in the coils of affliction;

entries.farsi: تن ز آتشهاى دل بگداخته / ‏خانه از غير خدا پرداخته
entries.english: His body melted by the fires of his heart; the house emptied of all but God.

entries.farsi: ديده هر ساعت دلش در اجتهاد / روز و شب چفسيده او بر اجتهاد
entries.english: The chastisement of pitiless tribulation brands on brands, so many thousands.

entries.farsi: گوشمال محنت بى‏زينهار / داغها بر داغها چندين هزار
entries.english: Every hour saw his heart engaged in the struggle: by day and night he was firmly attached to the struggle.

entries.farsi: سال و مه در خون و خاك آميخته / ‏صبر و حلمش نيم شب بگريخته
entries.english: During years and months he had been mixed up with dust and blood: (on seeing the slave) at midnight, his patience and forbearance fled.

entries.farsi: گفت زاهد در سبوها چيست آن / ‏گفت باده گفت آن كيست آن
entries.english: “What is that in the jugs?” asked the ascetic. “Wine,” replied the slave. “Whose wine?” he said.

entries.farsi: گفت آن آن فالن مير اجل / ‏گفت طالب را چنين باشد عمل
entries.english: He answered, “It belongs to so-and-so, the most honourable Amír.” He said, “Is the seeker’s work like this?

entries.farsi: طالب يزدان و آن گه عيش و نوش / ‏باده‏ى شيطان و آن گه نيم هوش
entries.english: To be a seeker of God, and then indulge in pleasure and drinking! To drink the Devil’s wine, and then be semi-intelligent!”

entries.farsi: هوش تو بى‏مى چنين پژمرده است / ‏هوشها بايد بر آن هوش تو بست
entries.english: Without wine your intelligence is so shabby that other intelligences must be tied to your intelligence.

entries.farsi: تا چه باشد هوش تو هنگام سكر / اى چو مرغى گشته صيد دام سكر
entries.english: Consider what your intelligence will be at the time when you are intoxicated, O you who like a bird have fallen a prey to the snare of intoxication.

sections.title_fa: حكايت ضياء دلق كه سخت دراز بود و برادرش شيخ اسالم تاج بلخ بغايت كوتاه بالا بود و اين شيخ ،اسالم از برادرش ضيا ننگ داشتى، ضيا در آمد به درس او و همه صدور بلخ حاضر به درس او ضيا خدمتى كرد و بگذشت، شيخ اسالم او را نيم قيامى كرد سرسرى، گفت آرى سخت درازى پاره اى در دزد

sections.title_en: Story of Ziyá-yi Dalq, who was very tall, while his brother, the Shaykh of Islam Táj of Balkh, was exceedingly short; and this Shaykh of Islam was ashamed of his brother Ziyá. Ziyá came to hear his brother’s lecture, at which all the leading men of Balkh were present. Ziyá made a bow and passed on. The Shaykh of Islam half rose in a negligent pounder,
Ziyá said, “Yes, you are very tall: take a bit off your height!”

entries.farsi: آن ضياء دلق خوش الهام بود / دادر آن تاج شيخ اسالم بود
entries.english: Ziyá-yi Dalq was a man of goodly inspiration: he was the brother of Táj, the Shaykh of Islam.

entries.farsi: تاج شيخ اسالم دار الملك بلخ / ‏بود كوته قد و كوچك همچو فرخ
entries.english: Táj, the Shaykhu ’l-Islam of the imperial city of Balkh, was short of stature and small as a chick.

entries.farsi: گر چه فاضل بود و فحل و ذو فنون / ‏اين ضيا اندر ظرافت بد فزون
entries.english: Though he was learned and eminent and accomplished, this Ziyá was superior in wit.

entries.farsi: او بسى كوته ضيا بى‏حد دراز / بود شيخ اسالم را صد كبر و ناز
entries.english: He was very short, while Ziyá was tall beyond measure: the Shaykh of Islam had a hundred arrogances and haughty airs.

entries.farsi: زين برادر عار و ننگش آمدى / ‏آن ضيا هم واعظى بد با هدى
entries.english: He felt ashamed of this brother and disgraced; yet Ziyá was a preacher in the way of salvation.

entries.farsi: روز محفل اندر آمد آن ضيا / بارگه پر قاضيان و اصفيا
entries.english: On the day of congregation Ziyá came in: the hall was filled with cadis and men distinguished.

entries.farsi: كرد شيخ اسالم از كبر تمام / ‏اين برادر را چنين نصف القيام
entries.english: In his complete arrogance the Shaykh of Islam half rose, in such a fashion, to his brother.

entries.farsi: گفت او را بس درازى بهر مزد / اندكى ز آن قد سروت هم بدزد
entries.english: Ziyá said to him, “You are very tall: take a little off your cypress-like stature in order to gain the reward.”

entries.farsi: پس ترا خود هوش كو يا عقل كو / تا خورى مى اى تو دانش را عدو
entries.english: The ascetic said, “How, then, have you the intelligence, how have you the understanding to drink wine, O enemy of knowledge?

entries.farsi: در تو نورى كى در آمد اى غوى / ‏تا تو بى‏هوشى و ظلمت جو شوى
entries.english: If your face is very beautiful, put some indigo on it; indigo on the face of an Abyssinian would be a laughing-stock.

entries.farsi: روت بس زيباست نيلى هم بكش / ‏ضحكه باشد نيل بر روى حبش
entries.english: When did any light enter into you, O misguided man that you should become a seeker of unconsciousness and darkness?

entries.farsi: سايه در روز است جستن قاعده / ‏در شب ابرى تو سايه جو شده
entries.english: It is the rule to seek the shadow during the day; you seek the shadow on a cloudy night.

entries.farsi: عاشقان را باده خون دل بود / چشمشان بر راه و بر منزل بود
entries.english: If wine is lawful as sustenance for the common folk, it is unlawful to those who seek the Beloved.

entries.farsi: گر حالل آمد پى قوت عوام / ‏طالبان دوست را آمد حرام
entries.english: The wine for the lovers is their heart’s blood: their eyes are upon the Way and upon the Destination.

entries.farsi: در چنين راه بيابان مخوف / ‏اين قالووز خرد با صد كسوف
entries.english: In this Way across the terrible wilderness the guide, Reason, suffers a hundred eclipses.

entries.farsi: خاك در چشم قالووزان زنى / ‏كاروان را هالك و گمره كنى
entries.english: If you throw dust in the eyes of the guides, you will cause the caravan to perish and lose the way.

entries.farsi: نان جو حقا حرام است و فسوس / ‏نفس را در پيش نه نان سبوس
entries.english: In truth, barley bread is unlawful and injurious to the carnal soul: set bread made of bran before it.

entries.farsi: دزد را تو دست ببريدن پسند / از بريدن عاجزى دستش ببند
entries.english: Keep in abject submission the enemy on the Way to God: do not place a pulpit for the robber, keep him on the gibbet.

entries.farsi: دشمن راه خدا را خوار دار / دزد را منبر منه بر دار دار
entries.english: Deem the amputation of the robber’s hand desirable: if you are unable to cut his hand off, bind it.

entries.farsi: گر نبندى دست او دست تو بست / ‏گر تو پايش نشكنى پايت شكست
entries.english: Unless you bind his hand, he will bind yours; unless you break his leg, he will break yours.

entries.farsi: تو عدو را مى دهى و نى شكر / بهر چه گو زهر خند و خاك خور
entries.english: You give the enemy wine and sugar-cane—for what reason? Bid him laugh venomously and eat earth.”

entries.farsi: زد ز غيرت بر سبو سنگ و شكست / ‏او سبو انداخت و از زاهد بجست
entries.english: In his indignation he hurled a stone at the jug and broke it: he let the jug fall and sprang away from the ascetic.

entries.farsi: رفت پيش مير و گفتش باده كو / ماجرا را گفت يك يك پيش او
entries.english: He went to the Amír, who said to him, “Where is the wine?” He related in his presence all that had happened, point by point.

sections.title_fa: رفتن امير خشم آلود براى گوشمال زاهد
sections.title_en: How the enraged Amír set out to punish the ascetic.

entries.farsi: مير چون آتش شد و بر جست راست / ‏گفت بنما خانه‏ى زاهد كجاست
entries.english: The Amír became like fire and jumped straight up. “Show me,” he cried, “where the ascetic’s house is,

entries.farsi: تا بدين گرز گران كوبم سرش / ‏آن سر بى‏دانش مادر غرش
entries.english: That I may pound his head with this heavy club— his ignorant whoreson head.

entries.farsi: او چه داند امر معروف از سگى / ‏طالب معروفى است و شهرگى
entries.english: What should he know about enjoining to do right? He is currishly seeking notoriety and fame,

entries.farsi: تا بدين سالوس خود را جا كند / تا به چيزى خويشتن پيدا كند
entries.english: In order that by means of this hypocrisy he may make a position for himself and somehow make himself conspicuous;

entries.farsi: كاو ندارد خود هنر الا همان / ‏كه تسلس مى‏كند با اين و آن
entries.english: For in truth he has no talent save this alone, that he plays the hypocrite to all and sundry.

entries.farsi: او اگر ديوانه است و فتنه كاو / داروى ديوانه باشد كير گاو
entries.english: If he is mad and bent on mischief, the cure for a madman is an ox-hide whip,

entries.farsi: تا كه شيطان از سرش بيرون رود / بى‏لت خربندگان خر چون رود
entries.english: So that the devil may go forth from his head: how should an ass go without the ass-drivers’ blows?”

entries.farsi: مير بيرون جست دبوسى به دست / ‏نيم شب آمد به زاهد نيم مست
entries.english: The Amír dashed out, with a mace in his hand: at midnight he came, half intoxicated, to the ascetic.

entries.farsi: خواست كشتن مرد زاهد را ز خشم / ‏مرد زاهد گشت پنهان زير پشم
entries.english: In his rage he wished to kill the ascetic, the ascetic hid beneath wool.

entries.farsi: مرد زاهد مى‏شنيد از مير آن / ‏زير پشم آن رسن تابان نهان
entries.english: The ascetic, hidden under the wool belonging to certain rope-makers, heard that from the Amír.

entries.farsi: گفت در رو گفتن زشتى مرد / آينه تاند كه رو را سخت كرد
entries.english: He said, “The mirror that has made its face hard can tell a man to his face that he is ugly.

entries.farsi: روى بايد آينه‏وار آهنين / تات گويد روى زشت خود ببين
entries.english: It needs a steel face, like a mirror, to say to you, ‘Behold your ugly face.’”

sections.title_fa: حكايت مات كردن دلقك سيد شاه ترمد را
sections.title_en: Story of Dalqak’s checkmating the Sayyid, the Shah of Tirmid.

entries.farsi: شاه با دلقك همى شطرنج باخت / ‏مات كردش زود خشم شه بتاخت
entries.english: The Shah was playing chess with Dalqak: he checkmated him: immediately the Shah’s anger burst out.

entries.farsi: گفت شه شه و آن شه كبرآورش / ‏يك يك از شطرنج مى‏زد بر سرش
entries.english: He cried, “Checkmate, checkmate!” and the haughty monarch threw the chessmen, one by one, at his head,

entries.farsi: كه بگير اينك شهت اى قلتبان / ‏صبر كرد آن دلقك و گفت الامان
entries.english: Saying, “Take it! Here is ‘checkmate’ for you, O scoundrel.” Dalqak restrained himself and said, “Mercy!”

entries.farsi: دست ديگر باختن فرمود مير / او چنان لرزان كه عور از زمهرير
entries.english: The Prince commanded him to play a second game: he was trembling like a naked man in bitter cold.

entries.farsi: باخت دست ديگر و شه مات شد / وقت شه شه گفتن و ميقات شد
entries.english: He played the second game, and the Shah was defeated: the time and moment for saying “checkmate, checkmate” arrived,

entries.farsi: بر جهيد آن دلقك و در كنج رفت / ‏شش نمد بر خود فگند از بيم تفت
entries.english: Dalqak jumped up and ran into a corner and in his fear hastily flung six rugs over himself.

entries.farsi: زير بالشها و زير شش نمد / خفت پنهان تا ز زخم شه رهد
entries.english: He lay hidden beneath cushions and six rugs that he might escape from the Shah’s blows.

entries.farsi: گفت شه هى هى چه كردى چيست اين / كى توان حق گفت جز زير لحاف
entries.english: The Shah exclaimed, “Hi, hi! What have you done? What is this?” He replied, “Checkmate, checkmate, checkmate, checkmate, O excellent Shah!

entries.farsi: ‏با تو اى خشم آور آتش سجاف / اى تو مات و من ز زخم شاه مات
entries.english: How can one tell the truth to you except under cover, O wrathful man who art wrapped in fire,

entries.farsi: وز لگد بر در زدن وز دار و گير / خلق بيرون جست زود از چپ و راست
entries.english: O you, who are defeated, while I, defeated by your Majesty’s blows, are crying ‘checkmate, checkmate’ under your house-furnishings?”

entries.farsi: ‏مى‏زنم شه شه به زير رختهات / چون محله پر شد از هيهاى مير
entries.english: When the quarter became filled with the furious shouts of the Amír and his kicking the door and holding and seizing,

entries.farsi: ‏كاى مقدم وقت عفو است و رضاست / مغز او خشك است و عقلش اين زمان
entries.english: The people quickly rushed out from left and right, crying, “O Prince, ’it is the hour for pardon and grace.

entries.farsi: ‏كمتر است از عقل و فهم كودكان / زهد و پيرى ضعف در ضعف آمده
entries.english: His brain is dry, and at this time his intelligence is inferior to the intelligence and understanding of children.

entries.farsi: كارها كرده نديده مزد كار / يا نبود آن كار او را خود گهر
entries.english: Asceticism and old age have come on the top of each other, and no revelation has been given to him in his asceticism.

entries.farsi: ‏و اندر آن زهدش گشادى ناشده / رنج ديده گنج ناديده ز يار
entries.english: He has suffered the pain but never seen the gain from the Beloved: he has done works but never seen the wages for his work.

entries.farsi: يا نيامد وقت پاداش از قدر / يا كه بود آن سعى چون سعى جهود
entries.english: Either the essential thing was not in his work at all, or by decree the hour for the reward has not yet arrived.

entries.farsi: يا جزا وابسته‏ى ميقات بود / مر و را درد و مصيبت اين بس است
entries.english: Either his labour has been like the labour of the Jews, or the recompense has been connected with the appointed season.

entries.farsi: ‏كه در اين وادى پر خون بى‏كس است / چشم پر درد و نشسته او به كنج
entries.english: For him it is sorrow and misfortune enough that he is without any one to succour him in this vale full of blood.

entries.farsi: ‏رو ترش كرده فرو افكنده لنج / نه يكى كحال كاو را غم خورد
entries.english: His eyes are painful, and he sits in a corner, sour-faced and chop-fallen.

entries.farsi: نى‏ش عقلى كه به كحلى پى برد / اجتهادى مى‏كند با حزر و ظن
entries.english: There is no oculist to sympathize with him, nor has he any intelligence that he should find the way to a eye salve.

entries.farsi: ‏كار در بوك است تا نيكو شدن / ز آن رهش دور است تا ديدار دوست
entries.english: He is making a effort with conjecture and opinion: the matter rests in ‘maybe’ till it turn out well.

entries.farsi: كاو نجويد سر رئيسيش آرزوست / ساعتى او با خدا اندر عتاب
entries.english: Far is the road he must travel ere he sees the Beloved, because he does not seek the head; his desire is headship.

entries.farsi: ‏كه نصيبم رنج آمد زين حساب / ساعتى با بخت خود اندر جدال
entries.english: At one time he is in reproach, saying, ‘The portion allotted to me from this calculation is pain.’

entries.farsi: ‏كه همه پران و ما ببريده بال / هر كه محبوس است اندر بو و رنگ
entries.english: At another time he is in a quarrel with his own luck, saying, ‘All are flying, and we have our wings cut off.’”

entries.farsi: ‏گر چه در زهد است باشد خوش تنگ / تا برون نايد از اين تنگين مناخ
entries.english: Whoever is imprisoned in scent and colour, his spirit is oppressed, though he is in asceticism.

entries.farsi: ‏كى شود خويش خوش و صدرش فراخ / زاهدان را در خال پيش از گشاد
entries.english: Until he comes forth from this narrow resting-place, how should his spirit be happy and his breast expanded with joy?

entries.farsi: كارد و استره نشايد هيچ داد / كز ضجر خود را بدراند شكم
entries.english: One should never give a knife or razor to ascetics in solitude before they have gained the spiritual revelation,

entries.farsi: ‏غصه‏ى آن بى‏مراديها و غم
entries.english: Since the anguish of disappointments and the grief would cause them to rip their bellies asunder from agitation of mind.

sections.title_fa: انداختن مصطفى عليه السلام خود را از كوه حرى از وحشت دير نمودن جبرئيل عليه السلام و ‏نمودن جبرئيل عليه السلام خود را به وى كه مينداز ترا دولتها در پيش است
sections.title_en: How Mustafá, on whom be peace, cast himself down from Mount Hira because of his distress at the long delay of Gabriel, on whom be peace, in appearing; and how Gabriel, on whom be peace,
showed himself to him, saying, “Do not cast yourself down, for fortunes are in front of you.”

entries.farsi: مصطفى را هجر چون بفراختى / ‏خويش را از كوه مى‏انداختى
entries.english: Whenever separation overpowered Mustafá, he would be on the point of casting himself down from the mountain,

entries.farsi: تا بگفتى جبرئيلش هين مكن / ‏كه ترا بس دولت است از امر كن
entries.english: Until Gabriel would say to him, “Listen, do not do it, for great fortune will come to you from the Command, Be!”

entries.farsi: مصطفى ساكن شدى ز انداختن / ‏باز هجران آوريدى تاختن
entries.english: Mustafá would desist from casting, separation again made an attack.

entries.farsi: باز خود را سر نگون از كوه او / مى‏فگندى از غم و اندوه او
entries.english: Again, from grief and sorrow, he would be on the point of throwing himself headlong from the mountain,

entries.farsi: باز خود پيدا شدى آن جبرئيل / ‏كه مكن اين اى تو شاه بى‏بديل
entries.english: And once more Gabriel would appear in person, saying, “Do it not, O you peerless king!”

entries.farsi: همچنين مى‏بود تا كشف حجاب / ‏تا بيابيد آن گهر را او ز جيب
entries.english: Even thus he would continue until the veil was lifted, so that he gained the pearl from the bosom.

entries.farsi: بهر هر محنت چو خود را مى‏كشند / اصل محنتهاست اين چونش كشند
entries.english: Since people kill themselves on account of any affliction, this is the root of afflictions: how should they endure it?

entries.farsi: از فدايى مردمان را حيرتى است / ‏هر يكى از ما فدايى سيرتى است
entries.english: Men are amazed at the Fida’i, every one of us is a Fida’i in his behaviour.

entries.farsi: اى خنك آن كه فدا كردست تن / ‏بهر آن كار زد فداى آن شدن
entries.english: Oh, blest is he that has sacrificed his body for the sake of that for which it is worthwhile to sacrifice one’s self.

entries.farsi: هر يكى چون كه فدايى فنى است / ‏كاندر آن ره صرف عمر و كشتنى است
entries.english: Inasmuch as everyone is devoted to some calling wherein he spends his life and is killed—

entries.farsi: بارى اين مقبل فداى اين فن است / ‏كاندر او صد زندگى در كشتن است
entries.english: Is killed somewhere in the West or in the East, at which time neither the desiring subject nor the desired object remains.

entries.farsi: كشتنى اندر غروبى يا شروق / ‏كه نه شايق ماند آن گه نه مشوق
entries.english: After all, this fortunate man is devoted to the calling wherein a hundred lives are in being killed;

entries.farsi: عاشق و معشوق و عشقش بر دوام / ‏در دو عالم بهره‏مند و نيك نام
entries.english: Its lover and beloved and love are everlastingly prosperous and renowned in both worlds.

entries.farsi: عفو كن اى مير بر سختى او / در نگر در درد و بد بختى او
entries.english: O my generous friends have pity on the votaries of passion: it is their business to go down to destruction after destruction.

entries.farsi: يا كرامى ارحموا اهل الهوى / ‏شانهم ورد التوى بعد التوى
entries.english: “O Amír, pardon his violence: consider his sorrow and ill-fortune,

entries.farsi: تا ز جرمت هم خدا عفوى كند / زلتت را مغفرت در آگند
entries.english: In order that God may pardon your sins likewise and heap forgiveness on your faults.

entries.farsi: تو ز غفلت بس سبو بشكسته‏اى / ‏در اميد عفو دل در بسته‏اى
entries.english: You have heedlessly broken many a jug and set your heart on the hope of pardon.

entries.farsi: عفو كن تا عفو يابى در جزا / مى‏شكافد مو قدر اندر سزا
entries.english: Pardon that you may win pardon in return: the decree splits hairs in deserts.”

sections.title_fa: جواب گفتن امير مر آن شفيعان را و همسايگان زاهد را كه گستاخى چرا كرد و سبوى ما را چرا ‏شكست من در اين باب شفاعت قبول نخواهم كرد كه سوگند خورده‏ام كه سزاى او را بدهم
sections.title_en: How the Amír answered those neighbours of the ascetic who interceded for him:
“Why,” said he, “did he behave impudently and why did he break my jug? I will not listen to intercession in this matter, for I have sworn to punish him as he deserves.”

entries.farsi: مير گفت او كيست كو سنگى زند / بر سبوى ما سبو را بشكند
entries.english: The Amír said, “Who is he that he should throw a stone at my jug and break it?

entries.farsi: چون گذر سازد ز كويم شير نر / ترس ترسان بگذرد با صد حذر
entries.english: When the fierce lion passes through my quarter, he passes in great affright and with a hundred precautions.

entries.farsi: بنده‏ى ما را چرا آزرد دل / ‏كرد ما را پيش مهمانان خجل
entries.english: Why did he vex the heart of my slave and put me to shame before my guests?

entries.farsi: شربتى كه به ز خون اوست ريخت / ‏اين زمان همچون زنان از ما گريخت
entries.english: He spilt a beverage that is better than his blood, and now he has fled from me, like women.

entries.farsi: ليك جان از دست من او كى برد / گير همچون مرغ بالا بر پرد
entries.english: But how shall he save his life from my hand? Suppose that he flies up on high like a bird,

entries.farsi: تير قهر خويش برپرش زنم / ‏پر و بال مرده ريگش بر كنم
entries.english: I will shoot the arrow of my vengeance at his wings; I will tear out his good for nothing wings and feathers.

entries.farsi: گر رود در سنگ سخت از كوششم / ‏از دل سنگش كنون بيرون كشم
entries.english: If he enters the hard rock from my pursuit, I will drag him forth from the heart of the rock just now.

entries.farsi: من برانم بر تن او ضربتى / ‏كه بود قوادگان را عبرتى
entries.english: I will inflict on his body such a blow as will be a warning to base scoundrels.

entries.farsi: با همه سالوس با ما نيز هم / ‏داد او و صد چو او اين دم دهم
entries.english: Hypocrisy to all and even to me! I will give him and a hundred like him their due at this moment.”

entries.farsi: خشم خون خوارش شده بد سركشى / ‏از دهانش مى‏برآمد آتشى
entries.english: His bloodthirsty wrath had become a rebel: a fire was coming up from his mouth.

sections.title_fa: دوم بار دست و پاى امير را بوسيدن و البه كردن شفيعان و همسايگان زاهد
sections.title_en: How the neighbours of the ascetic, who were interceding for him, kissed the hands and feet of the Amír and humbly entreated him a second time.

entries.farsi: آن شفيعان از دم هيهاى او / چند بوسيدند دست و پاى او
entries.english: At the breath of his clamour those intercessors kissed his hands and feet several times,

entries.farsi: كاى امير از تو نشايد كين كشى / گر بشد باده تو بى‏باده خوشى
entries.english: Saying, “O Amír, it does not beseem you to exact vengeance: if the wine is gone, you are delicious without wine.

entries.farsi: باده سرمايه ز لطف تو برد / لطف آب از لطف تو حسرت خورد
entries.english: Wine derives its original substance from your goodliness; the goodliness of water regrets your goodliness.

entries.farsi: پادشاهى كن ببخشش اى رحيم / ‏اى كريم ابن الكريم ابن الكريم
entries.english: Act royally; forgive him, O merciful one, O generous son of a generous sire and grandsire.

entries.farsi: هر شرابى بنده‏ى اين قد و خد / جمله مستان را بود بر تو حسد
entries.english: Every wine is the slave of this figure and cheek: all the drunk feel envy of you.

entries.farsi: هيچ محتاج مى گلگون نه‏اى / ‏ترك كن گلگونه تو گلگونه‏اى
entries.english: You have no need of rosy wine: take leave of rosiness, you are rosiness.

entries.farsi: اى رخ چون زهره‏ات شمس الضحى / اى گداى رنگ تو گلگونه‏ها
entries.english: O you whose Venus-like countenance is as the morning sun, O you of whose colour all rosiness is like a beggar,

entries.farsi: اى همه دريا چه خواهى كرد نم / ‏وى همه هستى چه مى‏جويى عدم
entries.english: The wine that is bubbling invisibly in the jar bubbles thus from longing for your face.

entries.farsi: باده كاندر خنب مى‏جوشد نهان / ‏ز اشتياق روى تو جوشد چنان
entries.english: O you who are the whole sea, what will you do with dew? And O you who are the whole of existence, why are you seeking non-existence?

entries.farsi: اى مه تابان چه خواهى كرد گرد / اى كه مه در پيش رويت روى زرد
entries.english: O resplendent Moon, what will you does with the dust, O you beside whose face the moon is pallid?

entries.farsi: تو خوش و خوبى و كان هر خوشى / ‏تو چرا خود منت باده كشى
entries.english: You are lovely and beautiful and the mine of every loveliness: why indeed should you lay yourself under obligations to wine?

entries.farsi: تاج كرمناست بر فرق سرت / ‏طوق أَعْ طَ يْناكَ آويز برت
entries.english: The tiara of We have honoured is on the crown of your head; the collar of We have given you hangs on your breast.

entries.farsi: جوهر است انسان و چرخ او را عرض / ‏جمله فرع و پايه‏اند و او غرض
entries.english: Man is the substance, and the celestial sphere is his accident; all things are a branch or the step of a ladder: he is the object.

entries.farsi: اى غالمت عقل و تدبيرات و هوش / ‏چون چنينى خويش را ارزان فروش
entries.english: O you to whom reason and foresight and intelligence are slaves, how are you selling yourself so cheaply?

entries.farsi: خدمتت بر جمله هستى مفترض / ‏جوهرى چون نجده خواهد از عرض
entries.english: Service to you is imposed on all existence as a duty: how should a substance beg for help from an accident?

entries.farsi: علم جويى از كتبها اى فسوس / ‏ذوق جويى تو ز حلوا اى فسوس
entries.english: You seek knowledge from books—oh, ridiculous! You seek pleasure from halwá—oh, ridiculous!

entries.farsi: بحر علمى در نمى پنهان شده / ‏در سه گز تن عالمى پنهان شده
entries.english: You are the sea of knowledge hidden in a dewdrop; you are the universe hidden in a body three ells long.

entries.farsi: آفتاب از ذره‏اى شد وام خواه / ‏زهره‏اى از خمره‏اى شد جام خواه
entries.english: What is wine or music or sexual intercourse that you should seek delight and profit there from?

entries.farsi: مى چه باشد يا سماع و يا جماع / ‏تا بجويى زو نشاط و انتفاع
entries.english: It is as though the sun sought to borrow from a mote, a Zuhra begged for a cup of wine from a small jar.

entries.farsi: جان بى‏كيفى شده محبوس كيف / ‏آفتابى حبس عقده اينت حيف
entries.english: You are the unconditional spirit imprisoned in conditionality, you are the sun imprisoned in the node: here’s a pity!”

sections.title_fa: باز جواب گفتن امير ايشان را
sections.title_en: How the Amír answered them again.

entries.farsi: گفت نه نه من حريف آن ميم / ‏من به ذوق اين خوشى قانع نيم
entries.english: He replied, “No, no, I am the fellow for that wine: I am not content with tasting this delight.

entries.farsi: من چنان خواهم كه همچون ياسمين / ‏كژ همى‏گردم چنان گاهى چنين
entries.english: I desire such wine, that, like the jasmine, I may ever be reeling crookedly that way, now this,

entries.farsi: وارهيده از همه خوف و اميد / كژ همى‏گردم به هر سو همچو بيد
entries.english: And, having been delivered from all fear and hope, I may be swaying to every side, like the willow,

entries.farsi: همچو شاخ بيد گردان چپ و راست / ‏كه ز بادش گونه گونه رقصهاست
entries.english: Swaying to left and right like the willow-bough, which is made to dance all sorts of dances by the wind.”

entries.farsi: آن كه خو كردست با شادى مى / ‏اين خوشى را كى پسندد خواجه هى
entries.english: He that is accustomed to the joy of wine, how should he be satisfied with this delight, Khwaja, eh?

entries.farsi: انبيا ز آن زين خوشى بيرون شدند / كه سرشته در خوشى حق بدند
entries.english: The prophets abandoned this delight because they were steeped in the Divine delight;

entries.farsi: با بت زنده كسى چون گشت يار / مرده را چون در كشد اندر كنار
entries.english: Since their spirit had experienced that delight, these delights seemed to them play.

entries.farsi: ز انكه جانشان آن خوشى را ديده بود / اين خوشيها پيششان بازى نمود
entries.english: When any one has been united with a living object of adoration, how should he embrace a dead one?

sections.title_fa: تفسير اين آيت كه وَ إِنَّ الدَّارَ الآْ خِرَةَ لَهِيَ الْحَيَوانُ لَوْ كانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ‏كه در و ديوار و عرصه‏ى آن عالم و آب و كوزه و ميوه و درخت همه زنده‏اند و سخن گوى و سخن شنو و جهت آن فرمود مصطفى عليه السلام كه الدنيا جيفه و طالبها كالب و اگر آخرت را حيات نبودى آخرت هم جيفه بودى، جيفه را براى مردگيش جيفه گويند نز براى بوى زشت و فرخجى

sections.title_en: Commentary on the Verse “And lo, the After-home is the life, if they but knew.” The gates and walls and area of that World and its water and pitchers and fruits and trees, all are living and speaking and hearing; and on that account Mustafá, on whom be peace, has said that the present world is a carcass and those who seek it are curs. If the next world had no life, the next world too would be a carcass: a carcass is so called because of its being dead,
not because of its evil smell and its foulness.

entries.farsi: آن جهان چون ذره ذره زنده‏اند / نكته دانند و سخن گوينده‏اند
entries.english: Since every atom of that World is living and able to understand discourse and eloquent,

entries.farsi: در جهان مرده‏شان آرام نيست / ‏كاين علف جز اليق انعام نيست
entries.english: They have no rest in the dead world, for this fodder is only fit for cattle.

entries.farsi: هر كه را گلشن بود بزم و وطن / ‏كى خورد او باده اندر گولخن
entries.english: Whoever has the rose-garden to feast and dwell in, how should he drink wine in the bath-stove?

entries.farsi: جاى روح پاك عليين بود / كرم باشد كش وطن سرگين بود
entries.english: The abode of the pure spirit is ‘Illiyyin; it is the worm that has its home in shit.

entries.farsi: بهر مخمور خدا جام طهور / بهر اين مرغان كور اين آب شور
entries.english: The cup that purifies is for those intoxicated with God; this briny water is for these blind birds.

entries.farsi: هر كه عدل عمرش ننمود دست / ‏پيش او حجاج خونى عادل است
entries.english: In the eyes of any one to whom the justice of ‘Umar has not displayed its power, the murderous Hajjáj is just.

entries.farsi: دختران را لعبت مرده دهند / كه ز لعب زندگان بى‏آگهند
entries.english: A dead doll is given to girls, for they are ignorant of the play of living men.

entries.farsi: چون ندارند از فتوت زور و دست / ‏كودكان را تيغ چوبين بهتر است
entries.english: A wooden sword is better suited to children, since they have not the strength and power from manhood.

entries.farsi: كافران قانع به نقش انبيا / كه نگاريده‏ست اندر ديرها
entries.english: Infidels are content with the figures of the prophets which are painted in churches;

entries.farsi: ز آن مهان ما را چو دور روشنى است / ‏هيچ‏مان پرواى نقش سايه نيست
entries.english: But as we have a bright period from those moons, we have no care for a shadow-figure.

entries.farsi: اين يكى نقشش نشسته در جهان / ‏و آن دگر نقشش چو مه در آسمان
entries.english: The one figure of him is seated in the world, while his other figure is in heaven, like the moon.

entries.farsi: اين دهانش نكته گويان با جليس / ‏و آن دگر با حق به گفتار و انيس
entries.english: This mouth of him is speaking on subtle points to those sitting beside him, while the other is in discourse with God and intimate.

entries.farsi: گوش ظاهر اين سخن را ضبط كن / ‏گوش جانش جاذب اسرار كن
entries.english: His outward ear is apprehending these words, while his spiritual ear is drawing the mysteries of Be.

entries.farsi: چشم ظاهر ضابط حليه‏ى بشر / چشم سر حيران ما زاغَ البصر
entries.english: His outward eye is apprehending human forms and features, while his inward eye is dazzled in the eye did not stray.

entries.farsi: پاى ظاهر در صف مسجد صواف / ‏پاى معنى فوق گردون در طواف
entries.english: His outward feet stand evenly in the row in the mosque, while his spiritual feet are in circumambulation above the sky.

entries.farsi: جزو جزوش را تو بشمر همچنين / ‏اين درون وقت و آن بيرون حين
entries.english: Reckon up every member of him in like fashion: this is within Time, while that is beyond Time.

entries.farsi: اين كه در وقت است باشد تا اجل / ‏و آن دگر يار ابد قرن ازل
entries.english: This which is in Time endures till death, while the other is the associate of everlastingness and the peer of eternity.

entries.farsi: خلوت و چله بر او الزم نماند / هيچ غيمى مر و را غايم نماند
entries.english: One name of him is “owner of the two empires”; one description of him is “Imam of the two qiblas.”

entries.farsi: هست يك نامش ولى الدولتين / ‏هست يك نعتش امام القبلتين
entries.english: The religious seclusion and the forty days’ fast are no longer incumbent on him: no cloud is clouding over him anymore.

entries.farsi: علت و پرهيز شد بحران نماند / كفر او ايمان شد و كفران نماند
entries.english: His solitary cell is the sun’s orb: how should alien night throw a veil over it?

entries.farsi: قرص خورشيد است خلوت خانه‏اش / ‏كى حجاب آرد شب بيگانه‏اش
entries.english: Sickness and abstinence are gone, the crisis is past: his infidelity has become faith, and disbelief is no more.

entries.farsi: چون الف از استقامت شد به پيش / ‏او ندارد هيچ از اوصاف خويش
entries.english: Like alif, he has taken the foremost place because of his straightness: he retains nothing of his own qualities.

entries.farsi: گشت فرد از كسوه‏ى خوهاى خويش / ‏شد برهنه جان به جان افزاى خويش
entries.english: He has become separated from the garment of his own dispositions: his spirit has gone, naked, to Him who gives it increase of spirituality.

entries.farsi: چون برهنه رفت پيش شاه فرد / شاهش از اوصاف قدسى جامه كرد
entries.english: Inasmuch as it went naked into the presence of the incomparable King, the King made for it a raiment of holy qualities.

entries.farsi: خلعتى پوشيد از اوصاف شاه / ‏بر پريد از چاه بر ايوان جاه
entries.english: It put on a robe of the King’s qualities: it flew up from the pit to the palace of majesty.

entries.farsi: اين چنين باشد چو دردى صاف گشت / ‏از بن طشت آمد او بالاى طشت
entries.english: Such is the case: when dregs become pure, they rise from the bottom of the bowl to the top.

entries.farsi: در بن طشت ار چه بود او دردناك / ‏شومى آميزش اجزاى خاك
entries.english: Although it remained like dregs at the bottom of the bowl owing to the ill-luck of mixing with particles of earth.

entries.farsi: يار ناخوش پر و بالش بسته بود / ور نه او در اصل بس برجسته بود
entries.english: Its disagreeable companion had tied its wings and plumes; else originally it was very soaring.

entries.farsi: چون عتاب اهْبِطُ وا انگيختند / همچو هاروتش نگون آويختند
entries.english: When they uttered the rebuke Get ye down, they suspended it, head first, like Harut.

entries.farsi: بود هاروت از مالك آسمان / ‏از عتابى شد معلق همچنان
entries.english: Harut was one of the angels of Heaven: on account of a rebuke he was suspended thus.

entries.farsi: سر نگون ز آن شد كه از سر دور ماند / خويش را سر ساخت و تنها پيش راند
entries.english: He was, head downwards, because he remained far aloof from the Head and made himself the head and advanced alone.

entries.farsi: آن سبد خود را چو پر از آب ديد / كرد استغنا و از دريا بريد
entries.english: When the basket saw itself to be full of water, it behaved with independence and parted from the sea.

entries.farsi: بر جگر آبش يكى قطره نماند / بحر رحمت كرد و او را باز خواند
entries.english: When, not a single drop of water remained inside it, the sea showed mercy and called it back.

entries.farsi: رحمتى بى‏علتى بى‏خدمتى / ‏آيد از دريا مبارك ساعتى
entries.english: From the Sea comes an uncaused undeserved mercy in a blessed hour.

entries.farsi: اللهَّ اللهَّ گرد دريا بار گرد / گر چه باشند اهل دريا بار زرد
entries.english: For God’s sake, for God’s sake, frequent the Seashore, though those who dwell on the seashore are pale,

entries.farsi: تا كه آيد لطف بخشايش‏گرى / ‏سرخ گردد روى زرد از گوهرى
entries.english: In order that the grace of a Benefactor may come and that your pale face may be reddened by a jewel.

entries.farsi: زردى رو بهترين رنگهاست / ‏ز انكه اندر انتظار آن لقاست
entries.english: Yellowness of face is the best of complexions, because it is in expectation of that meeting;

entries.farsi: ليك سرخى بر رخى كان المع است / ‏بهر آن آمد كه جانش قانع است
entries.english: But the redness on a face that is beaming appears because his soul is content;

entries.farsi: كه طمع الغر كند زرد و ذليل / ‏نيست او از علت ابدان عليل
entries.english: For hope makes him lean, pale, and wretched: he is not ill with bodily ailment.

entries.farsi: چون ببيند روى زرد بى‏سقم / ‏خيره گردد عقل جالينوس هم
entries.english: The reason of even Galen becomes distraught when it sees a pale face without disease.

entries.farsi: چون طمع بستى تو در انوار هو / ‏مصطفى گويد كه ذلت نفسه
entries.english: When you have fixed your hope on the Light of Him, Mustafá says, “His carnal self is abased.”

entries.farsi: نور بى‏سايه لطيف و عالى است / ‏آن مشبك سايه‏ى غربالى است
entries.english: The shadeless light is beautiful and lofty; the one enclosed in network is the shadow of a sieve.

entries.farsi: عاشقان عريان همى‏خواهند تن / ‏پيش عنينان چه جامه چه بدن
entries.english: Lovers desire to be naked of body; to those who are impotent what difference is there between a body and (one covered with) a robe?

entries.farsi: روزه داران را بود آن نان و خوان / ‏خر مگس را چه ابا چه ديگدان
entries.english: The bread and dishes of food are for the fasters; for the horse-fly what difference is there between the soup and the trivet?

sections.title_fa: دگر بار استدعاى شاه از اياز كه تاويل كار خود بگو و مشكل منكران را و طاعنان را حل كن كه ‏ايشان را در آن التباس رها كردن مروت نيست
sections.title_en: How the King requested Ayaz for the second time, saying, “Explain your case and solve the difficulty
felt by the incredulous and censorious; for it is not generosity to leave them in perplexity.”

entries.farsi: اين سخن از حد و اندازه‏ست بيش / ‏اى اياز اكنون بگو احوال خويش
entries.english: This topic is beyond limit and measure. “Now, O Ayaz, tell of your ‘states.’

entries.farsi: هست احوال تو از كان نوى / ‏تو بدين احوال كى راضى شوى
entries.english: Your states’ are from the mine of novelty” how should you be satisfied with these ‘states’?

entries.farsi: هين حكايت كن از آن احوال خوش / ‏خاك بر احوال و درس پنج و شش
entries.english: Listen, tell the story of those goodly ‘states’- dust upon the ‘states’ and lessons of the five and the six directions!”

entries.farsi: حال باطن گر نمى‏آيد به گفت / ‏حال ظاهر گويمت در طاق و جفت
entries.english: If the inward “state” is not to be told, I will tell you the outward “state” in a word or two,

entries.farsi: كه ز لطف يار تلخيهاى مات / ‏گشت بر جان خوشتر از شكر نبات
entries.english: That by grace of the Beloved the bitterness of death was made sweeter than sugar-cane to the soul.

entries.farsi: حال هر روزى به دى مانند نى / ‏همچو جو اندر روش كش بند نى
entries.english: If the dust from that sugar-cane should enter the sea, all the bitterness of the sea would become sweet.

entries.farsi: ز آن نبات ار گرد در دريا رود / تلخى دريا همه شيرين شود
entries.english: Even so a hundred thousand “states” came and went back to the Unseen, O trusted one.

entries.farsi: صد هزار احوال آمد همچنين / ‏باز سوى غيب رفتند اى امين
entries.english: Each day’s “state” is not like the day before: as a river that hath no obstacle in its course.

entries.farsi: شادى هر روز از نوعى دگر / فكرت هر روز را ديگر اثر
entries.english: Each day’s joy is of a different kind, each day’s thought makes a different impression.

sections.title_fa: تمثيل تن آدمى به مهمان خانه و انديشه‏هاى مختلف به مهمانان مختلف، و عارف در رضا بدان انديشه‏هاى غم و شادى چون شخص مهمان دوست غريب نو از خليل‏وار، كه در خليل به اكرام ضيف ‏پيوسته باز بود بر كافر و مومن و امين و خاين و با همه مهمانان رو تازه داشتى

sections.title_en: Comparison of the human body to a guest-house and of the diverse thoughts to the diverse guests. The gnostic, acquiescing in those thoughts of sorrow or joy, resembles a hospitable person who treats strangers with kindness., like Khalil; for Khalil’s door was always open to receive his guest with honour- infidel and true believer and trusty and treacherous alike;
and he would show a cheerful face to all his guests.

entries.farsi: هست مهمان خانه اين تن اى جوان / ‏هر صباحى ضيف نو آيد دوان
entries.english: This body, O youth, is a guest house: every morning a new guest comes running.

entries.farsi: هر چه آيد از جهان غيب‏وش / ‏در دلت ضيف است او را دار خوش
entries.english: Beware, do not say, “This is a burden to me,” for presently he will fly back into non-existence.

entries.farsi: هين مگو كاين ماند اندر گردنم / ‏كه هم اكنون باز پرد در عدم
entries.english: Whatever comes into your heart from the invisible world is your guest: entertain it well!

sections.title_fa: حكايت آن مهمان كه زن خداوند خانه گفت كه باران فرو گرفت و مهمان در گردن ما ماند
sections.title_en: Story of the guest concerning whom the wife of the master of the house said, “The rain has set in,
and our guest is left on our hands.”

entries.farsi: آن يكى را بى‏گهان آمد قنق / ‏ساخت او را همچو طوق اندر عنق
entries.english: A guest came to a certain man at a late hour: he made him like a collar on the neck.

entries.farsi: خوان كشيد او را كرامتها نمود / آن شب اندر كوى ايشان سور بود
entries.english: He brought trays of food and showed him every courtesy; on that night there was a feast in their parish.

entries.farsi: مرد زن را گفت پنهانى سخن / ‏كامشب اى خاتون دو جامه‏ى خواب كن
entries.english: The man spoke secretly to his wife, saying, “To-night, mistress, make two beds.”

entries.farsi: بستر ما را بگستر سوى در / بهر مهمان گستر آن سوى دگر
entries.english: Lay our bed towards the door, and lay a bed on the other side for the guest.”

entries.farsi: گفت زن خدمت كنم شادى كنم / ‏سمع و طاعه اى دو چشم روشنم
entries.english: The wife replied, “I will do service, I shall be glad. To hear is to obey, O light of mine eyes!”

entries.farsi: هر دو بستر گستريد و رفت زن / ‏سوى ختنه سور كرد آن جا وطن
entries.english: The wife laid both the beds and went off to the circumcision feast and stayed there.

entries.farsi: اند مهمان عزيز و شوهرش / ‏نقل بنهادند از خشك و ترش
entries.english: The worthy guest and her husband remained: the host set before him a dessert of fruit and wine.

entries.farsi: در سمر گفتند هر دو منتجب / ‏سر گذشت نيك و بد تا نيم شب
entries.english: Both the excellent men related their good and bad experiences chatting till midnight.

entries.farsi: بعد از آن مهمان ز خواب و از سمر / شد در آن بستر كه بود آن سوى در
entries.english: Afterwards the guest, being sleepy and tired of talking, went to the bed that was on the opposite side to the door.

entries.farsi: شوهر از خجلت بدو چيزى نگفت / ‏كه ترا اين سوست اى جان جاى خفت
entries.english: From shame the husband did not tell him anything or say, “My dear friend, your bed is on this side;

entries.farsi: آن قرارى كه به زن او داده بود / گشت مبدل و آن طرف مهمان غنود
entries.english: I have had the bed for you to sleep in laid over there, most noble sir.”

entries.farsi: ه براى خواب تو اى بو الكرم / ‏بستر آن سوى دگر افكنده‏ام
entries.english: The arrangement which he had made with his wife was altered, and the guest lay down on the other side.

entries.farsi: آن شب آن جا سخت باران در گرفت / ‏كز غليظى ابرشان آمد شگفت
entries.english: During the night it began to rain violently in that place, that they were astonished at the thickness of the clouds.

entries.farsi: زن بيامد بر گمان آن كه شو / سوى در خفته‏ست و آن سو آن عمو
entries.english: When the wife came home, she thought her husband was sleeping towards the door, and the uncle on the other side.

entries.farsi: رفت عريان در لحاف آن دم عروس / ‏داد مهمان را به رغبت چند بوس
entries.english: The wife immediately undressed and went to bed and kissed the guest fondly several times.

entries.farsi: گفت مى‏ترسيدم اى مرد كالن / ‏خود همان آمد همان آمد همان
entries.english: “O worthy man,” said she, “I was afraid, and now that very thing has happened, that very thing has happened, that very thing!

entries.farsi: مرد مهمان را گل و باران نشاند / بر تو چون صابون سلطانى بماند
entries.english: The mud and rain have stranded your guest: he is left on your hands like Government soap.

entries.farsi: اندر اين باران و گل او كى رود / بر سر و جان تو او تاوان شود
entries.english: How can he set out in this rain and mud? He will become a tax upon your head and soul.”

entries.farsi: زود مهمان جست و گفت اى زن بهل / ‏موزه دارم غم ندارم من ز گل
entries.english: The guest at once jumped up and said, “O woman, leave off! I have boots, I don’t mind the mud.

entries.farsi: من روان گشتم شما را خير باد / در سفر يك دم مبادا روح شاد
entries.english: I depart. May good be with you! May your spirit during its journey never rejoice for a moment,

entries.farsi: تا كه زوتر جانب معدن رود / كاين خوشى اندر سفر ره زن شود
entries.english: So that it may the sooner go towards its native home! For this pleasure waylays on his journey.”

entries.farsi: زن پشيمان شد از آن گفتار سرد / چون رميد و رفت آن مهمان فرد
entries.english: When the distinguished guest started up and went off, the wife was sorry for those unsympathetic words.

entries.farsi: زن بسى گفتش كه آخر اى امير / گر مزاحى كردم از طيبت مگير
entries.english: Many a time the wife said to him, “Why, O Amír, if I made a merry jest, don’t take offence.”

entries.farsi: سجده و زارى زن سودى نداشت / ‏رفت و ايشان را در آن حسرت گذاشت
entries.english: The wife’s supplication and lament were of no avail: he departed and left them to grieve.

entries.farsi: جامه از رق كرد ز آن پس مرد و زن / ‏صورتش ديدند شمعى بى‏لگن
entries.english: Afterwards the husband and wife clad themselves in blue: they deemed his form to be a candle without a basin.

entries.farsi: مى‏شد و صحرا ز نور شمع مرد / چون بهشت از ظلمت شب گشته فرد
entries.english: He was going, and by that man’s candle-light the desert was isolated, like Paradise, from the darkness of night.

entries.farsi: كرد مهمان خانه خانه‏ى خويش را / از غم و از خجلت اين ماجرا
entries.english: He made his house a guest-house in sorrow and shame for this event.

entries.farsi: در درون هر دو از راه نهان / ‏هر زمان گفتى خيال ميهمان
entries.english: In the hearts of them both, by the hidden way, the phantom of the guest was saying continually,

entries.farsi: كه منم يار خضر صد گنج جود / مى‏فشاندم ليك روزيتان نبود
entries.english: “I am the friend of Khadir: I would have scattered a hundred treasures of munificence, but it was not your appointed portion.”

sections.title_fa: تمثيل فكر هر روزينه كه اندر دل آيد به مهمان نو كه از اول روز در خانه فرو آيد و تحكم و بد خويى ‏كند به خداوند خانه و فضيلت مهمان نوازى و ناز مهمان كشيدن
sections.title_en: Comparing the daily thoughts that come into the heart with the new guests who from the beginning of the day alight in the house and behave with arrogance and ill-temper to­
wards the master of the house; and concerning the merit of treating the guest with kindness and of suffering his haughty airs patiently.

entries.farsi: فكر را اى جان به جاى شخص دان / ‏ز انكه شخص از فكر دارد قدر و جان
entries.english: Every day, too, at every moment a thought comes, like an honoured guest, into your bosom.

entries.farsi: هر دمى فكرى چو مهمان عزيز / آيد اندر سينه‏ات هر روز نيز
entries.english: O soul, regard thought as a person, since person derives his worth from thought and spirit.

entries.farsi: فكر غم گر راه شادى مى‏زند / كارسازيهاى شادى مى‏كند
entries.english: If the thought of sorrow is waylaying joy, it is making preparations for joy.

entries.farsi: خانه مى‏روبد به تندى او ز غير / تا در آيد شادى نو ز اصل خير
entries.english: It violently sweeps your house clear of else, in order that new joy from the source of good may enter in.

entries.farsi: مى‏فشاند برگ زرد از شاخ دل / ‏تا برويد برگ سبز متصل
entries.english: It scatters the yellow leaves from the bough of the heart, in order that incessant green leaves may grow.

entries.farsi: مى‏كند بيخ سروركهنه را / تا خرامد ذوق نو از ما و را
entries.english: It uproots the old joy, in order that new delight may march in from the Beyond.

entries.farsi: غم كند بيخ كژ پوسيده را / تا نمايد بيخ رو پوشيده را
entries.english: Sorrow pulls up the crooked rotten, in order that it may disclose the root that is veiled from sight.

entries.farsi: غم ز دل هر چه بريزد يا برد / در عوض حقا كه بهتر آورد
entries.english: Whatever sorrow may cause to be shed from the heart or may take away, assuredly it will bring better in exchange,

entries.farsi: خاصه آن را كه يقينش باشد اين / ‏كه بود غم بنده‏ى اهل يقين
entries.english: Especially for him who knows with certainty that sorrow is the servant of the possessors of certainty.

entries.farsi: گر ترش رويى نيارد ابر و برق / ‏رز بسوزد از تبسمهاى شرق
entries.english: Unless the clouds and the lightning show a frowning aspect, the vines will be burnt by the smiles of the sun.

entries.farsi: سعد و نحس اندر دلت مهمان شود / چون ستاره خانه خانه مى‏رود
entries.english: Good and ill fortune become guests in your heart: like the star, they go from house to house.

entries.farsi: آن زمان كه او مقيم برج تست / ‏باش همچون طالعش شيرين و چست
entries.english: At the time when it is residing in your mansion, adapt yourself to it and be agreeable, like its ascendant,

entries.farsi: تا كه با مه چون شود او متصل / ‏شكر گويد از تو با سلطان دل
entries.english: So that, when it rejoins the Moon, it may speak gratefully of you to the Lord of the heart.

entries.farsi: هفت سال ايوب با صبر و رضا / در بال خوش بود با ضيف خدا
entries.english: Job, the patient and well-pleased, showed sweetness to God’s guest during seven years in tribulation,

entries.farsi: تا چو واگردد بالى سخت رو / پيش حق گويد به صد گون شكر او
entries.english: To the end that when the stern-visaged tribulation should turn back, it might give thanks to him in God’s presence in a hundred fashions,

entries.farsi: كز محبت با من محبوب كش / ‏رو نكرد ايوب يك لحظه ترش
entries.english: Saying, “From love Job never for one moment looked sourly on me, the killer of that which is loved.”

entries.farsi: از وفا و خجلت علم خدا / بود چون شير و عسل او با بال
entries.english: From his loyalty and his shame before God’s knowledge, Job was like milk and honey towards tribulation.

entries.farsi: فكر در سينه در آيد نو به نو / خند خندان پيش او تو باز رو
entries.english: The thought comes into your breast anew; go to meet it with smiles and laughter,

entries.farsi: آن ضمير رو ترش را پاس دار / آن ترش را چون شكر شيرين شمار
entries.english: Saying, “O my Creator, preserve me from its evil: do not deprive me, let me partake, of its good!

entries.farsi: رب اوزعنى لشكر ما ارى / ‏ال تعقب حسره لى ان مضى
entries.english: O my Lord, prompt me to give thanks for that which I receive: do not let me feel any subsequent regret, if it shall pass away.”

entries.farsi: كه اعدنى خالقى من شره / ‏ال تحرمنى انل من بره
entries.english: Pay watchful regard to the sour-looking thought: deem that sour one to be sweet as sugar.

entries.farsi: ابر را گر هست ظاهر رو ترش / ‏گلشن آرنده‏ست ابر و شوره كش
entries.english: If the cloud apparently has a sour face, the cloud is the bringer-on of the rose-garden and the destroyer of the barren soil.

entries.farsi: فكر غم را تو مثال ابر دان / ‏با ترش تو رو ترش كم كن چنان
entries.english: Know that the thought of sorrow is like the cloud: do not look so sourly on the sour!

entries.farsi: بو كه آن گوهر به دست او بود / جهد كن تا از تو او راضى رود
entries.english: It may be that the pearl is in its hand: endeavour that it may depart from you well-pleased.

entries.farsi: ور نباشد گوهر و نبود غنى / ‏عادت شيرين خود افزون كنى
entries.english: And if the pearl is not and it is not rich, you will increase your sweet habit.

entries.farsi: جاى ديگر سود دارد عادتت / ‏ناگهان روزى بر آيد حاجتت
entries.english: Your habit will profit you on another occasion: someday your need will suddenly be fulfilled.

entries.farsi: فكرتى كز شاديت مانع شود / آن به امر و حكمت صانع شود
entries.english: The thought that hinders you from joy comes by the command and wise purpose of the Maker.

entries.farsi: تو مخوان دو چار دانگش اى جوان / ‏بو كه نجمى باشد و صاحب قران
entries.english: O youth, do not call it worthless: it may be a star and endowed with imperial fortune.

entries.farsi: تو مگو فرعى است او را اصل گير / تا بوى پيوسته بر مقصود چير
entries.english: Do not say it is a branch: take it to be the root, in order that you may always be master of your object of desire;

entries.farsi: ور تو آن را فرع گيرى و مضر / چشم تو در اصل باشد منتظر
entries.english: For if you take it to be a branch and pernicious, your eye will be waiting to see the root.

entries.farsi: زهر آمد انتظار اندر چشش / ‏دايما در مرگ باشى ز ان روش
entries.english: Waiting to see is poison to perception: by that method you will remain perpetually in death.

entries.farsi: اصل دان آن را بگيرش در كنار / باز ره دايم ز مرگ انتظار
entries.english: Recognise it as the root, clasp it to your bosom, and be forever delivered from the death of waiting to see.

sections.title_fa: نواختن سلطان اياز را
sections.title_en: How the Sultan showed favour to Ayaz.

entries.farsi: اى اياز پر نياز صدق كيش / ‏صدق تو از بحر و از كوه است بيش
entries.english: “O Ayaz, who is full of humbleness and sincere in all your ways, your sincerity is mightier than sea and mountain.

entries.farsi: نه به وقت شهوتت باشد عثار / كه رود عقل چو كوهت كاه‏وار
entries.english: For you there is no stumbling in the hour of lust, so that your reason, which resembles a mountain, should go like a straw;

entries.farsi: نه به وقت خشم و كينه صبرهات / ‏سست گردد در قرار و در ثبات
entries.english: Nor in the hour of anger and vengeance do your powers of long-suffering fail to hold fast and firm.”

entries.farsi: مردى اين مردى است نه ريش و ذكر / ور نه بودى شاه مردان كير خر
entries.english: Masculinity is this (kind of) manliness, not (having) a beard and a penis; otherwise, an ass’s penis would be the king of men

entries.farsi: حق كه را خوانده‏ست در قرآن رجال / ‏كى بود اين جسم را آن جا مجال
entries.english: Whom has God in the Qur’an called men? How should there be room for this body there?

entries.farsi: روح حيوان را چه قدر است اى پدر / آخر از بازار قصابان گذر
entries.english: What worth has the animal soul? O father, come now, pass through the market of the butchers,

entries.farsi: روسپى باشد كه از جوالن كير / عقل او موشى شود شهوت چو شير
entries.english: A hundred thousand heads laid on paunches (tripe), of which heads the value is less than the fat hind part and tail.

entries.farsi: صد هزاران سر نهاده بر شكم / ‏ارزشان از دنبه و از دنب كم
entries.english: A prostitute is (a woman) whose intellect becomes (weak as) a mouse, (her) lust (strong as) a lion from the motion of a penis.

sections.title_fa: ‏وصيت كردن پدر دختر را كه خود را نگه دار تا حامله نشوى از شوهرت
sections.title_en: How a father enjoined his daughter to take care lest she should become with child by her husband.

entries.farsi: خواجه‏اى بوده‏ست او را دخترى / ‏زهره خدى مه رخى سيمين برى
entries.english: There was a Khwaja who had a daughter with cheeks like Venus, a face like the moon, and a breast as silver.

entries.farsi: گشت بالغ داد دختر را به شو / شو نبود اندر كفايت كفو او
entries.english: When she reached maturity, he gave his daughter to a husband: as regards social rank the husband was not a match for her.

entries.farsi: خربزه چون در رسد شد آبناك / ‏گر بنشكافى تلف گشت و هالك
entries.english: When a melon is ripe it becomes watery and goes to waste and ruin unless you slice it.

entries.farsi: چون ضرورت بود دختر را بداد / او به ناكفوى ز تخويف فساد
entries.english: Since it was necessary, he gave his daughter to one who was not her match, in fear of the evil.

entries.farsi: گفت دختر را كز اين داماد نو / خويشتن پرهيز كن حامل مشو
entries.english: He said to his daughter, “Guard yourself from this new bridegroom, do not become with child;

entries.farsi: كه ضرورت بود عقد اين گدا / اين غريب اشمار را نبود وفا
entries.english: For your marriage to this beggar was by necessity; there is no constancy in this vagabond fellow.

entries.farsi: ناگهان بجهد كند ترك همه / ‏بر تو طفل او بماند مظلمه
entries.english: Of a sudden he will jump off and leave all behind: his child will remain on your hands as a wrong.”

entries.farsi: گفت دختر كاى پدر خدمت كنم / ‏هست پندت دل پذير و مغتنم
entries.english: The daughter replied, “O father, I will do service: your counsel is acceptable and prized.”

entries.farsi: حامله شد ناگهان دختر از او / چون بود هر دو جوان خاتون و شو
entries.english: Every two or three days the father would enjoin his daughter to take precautions;

entries.farsi: هر دو روزى هر سه روزى آن پدر / دختر خود را بفرمودى حذر
entries.english: She suddenly became with child by him: how should it be both the wife and the husband are young?

entries.farsi: از پدر او را خفى مى‏داشتش / ‏پنج ماهه گشت كودك يا كه شش
entries.english: She kept the child hidden from her father, till the child was five or six months old.

entries.farsi: گشت پيدا گفت بابا چيست اين / ‏من نگفتم كه از او دورى گزين
entries.english: The discovery was made. “What is this?” asked her father; “did not I tell you to adopt withdrawal from him?

entries.farsi: اين وصيتهاى من خود باد بود / كه نكردت پند و وعظم هيچ سود
entries.english: Truly my injunctions were wind! My counsel and exhortations have been of no use to you.”

entries.farsi: گفت بابا چون كنم پرهيز من / ‏آتش و پنبه است بى‏شك مرد و زن
entries.english: “Father,” said she, “how should I guard myself? Man and wife, beyond doubt, are fire and cotton.

entries.farsi: پنبه را پرهيز از آتش كجاست / ‏يا در آتش كى حفاظ است و تقاست
entries.english: What means has the cotton of guarding itself from the fire, or when is there carefulness and caution in the fire?”

entries.farsi: در زمان حال و انزال و خوشى / ‏خويشتن بايد كه از وى در كشى
entries.english: He replied, “I said, do not go near him, and do not allow his seed to enter you.

entries.farsi: گفت كى دانم كه انزالش كى است / ‏اين نهان است و به غايت دور دست
entries.english: She said, how could I know the moment of climax?

entries.farsi: گفت من گفتم كه سوى او مرو / تو پذيراى منى او مشو
entries.english: It is hidden and difficult to anticipate’”

entries.farsi: گفت تا چشمش كالپيسه شدن / ‏كور گشته است اين دو چشم كور من
entries.english: He replied, “When his eyes have the look of daggers, it is the time for ejaculation.”

entries.farsi: گفت چشمش چون كالپيسه شود / فهم كن كان وقت انزالش بود
entries.english: She said, “Until his eyes begin to look daggers my own eyes are blind and closed in passion.”

entries.farsi: نيست هر عقلى حقيرى پايدار / وقت حرص و وقت خشم و كارزار
entries.english: Not every despicable understanding remains steadfast in the hour of desire and anger and combat.

sections.title_fa: وصف ضعيف دلى و سستى صوفى سايه پرورد مجاهده ناكرده‏ى درد و داغ عشق ناچشيده به سجده و دست بوس عام و به حرمت نظر كردن و به انگشت نمودن ايشان كه امروز در زمانه صوفى اوست غره شده و به وهم بيمار شده همچو آن معلم كه كودكان گفتند كه رنجورى و با اين وهم كه من مجاهدم مرا در اين ره پهلوان مى‏دانند با غازيان به غزا رفته كه به ظاهر نيز هنر بنمايم در جهاد اكبر مستثناام جهاد اصغر خود پيش من چه محل دارد خيال شير ديده و دليريها كرده و مست َّاين دليرى شده و روى به بيشه نهاده به قصد شير و شير به زبان حال گفته كه كَلاَّ سَ وْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ ثُم
َكَلاَّ سَ وْفَ تَعْلَمُون
sections.title_en: Description of the weak spirit of the Sufi who has been brought up in ease and has never struggled with himself or experienced the pain and searing anguish of love, and has been deluded by the homage and hand-kissing of the common and their gazing on him with veneration and pointing at him with their fingers and saying, “He is the most famous Sufi in the world to-day”;

and has been made sick by vain imagination, like the teacher who was told by the children that he was ill. In the conceit of being a warrior and regarded as a hero in this Way, he goes on campaign with the soldiers engaged in the war against the infidels. “I will show my valour outwardly too,” says he; “I am unparalleled in the Greater Warfare: what difficulty, in truth, should the Lesser Warfare present to me?” He has beheld the phantasm of a lion and performed imaginary feats of bravery and become intoxicated with this bravery and has set out for the jungle to seek the lion. The lion says with mute eloquence, “No, you will see! And again, no, you will see!”

entries.farsi: رفت يك صوفى به لشكر در غزا / ناگهان آمد قطاريق و وغا
entries.english: A Sufi went with the army to fight the infidels: suddenly came the clangours and din of war.

entries.farsi: ماند صوفى با بنه و خيمه و ضعاف / ‏فارسان راندند تا صف مصاف
entries.english: The Sufi stayed behind with the baggage-train and tents and invalids, the horsemen rode into the line of battle.

entries.farsi: مثقالن خاك بر جا ماندند / سابقون السابقون در راندند
entries.english: The earth-bound heavies remained in their place; the foremost in the march, the foremost in the march, rode on.

entries.farsi: جنگها كرده مظفر آمدند / باز گشته با غنايم سودمند
entries.english: After the combat, they came victorious: they returned in possession of profit and with spoils.

entries.farsi: ارمغان دادند كاى صوفى تو نيز / او برون انداخت نستد هيچ چيز
entries.english: They gave a present, saying, “You too, O Sufi!” he cast it out and would not take anything.

entries.farsi: پس بگفتندش كه خشمينى چرا / گفت من محروم ماندم از غزا
entries.english: Then they said to him, “Why are you angry?” He answered, “I have been deprived of the fighting.”

entries.farsi: ز آن تلطف هيچ صوفى خوش نشد / كه ميان غزو خنجر كش نشد
entries.english: The Sufi was not at all pleased with that act of kindness, because he had not drawn the sword in the holy war.

entries.farsi: پس بگفتندش كه آورديم اسير / آن يكى را بهر كشتن تو بگير
entries.english: So they said to him, “We have brought prisoners in: do you take that one to kill.

entries.farsi: سر ببرش تا تو هم غازى شوى / اندكى خوش گشت صوفى دل قوى
entries.english: Cut off his head, in order that you too may be a holy warrior.” The Sufi was somewhat pleased and encouraged;

entries.farsi: كآب را گر در وضو صد روشنى است / ‏چون كه آن نبود تيمم كردنى است
entries.english: For, though the ritual ablution water has a hundred excellences, when it is not obtainable one must make use of sand.

entries.farsi: برد صوفى آن اسير بسته را / در پس خرگه كه آرد او غزا
entries.english: The Sufi led the pinioned prisoner behind the tent in order to wage the holy war.

entries.farsi: دير ماند آن صوفى آن جا با اسير / قوم گفتا دير ماند آن جا فقير
entries.english: The Sufi tarried with the prisoner a long while; the party said, “The dervish has made a long stay there.

entries.farsi: كافر بسته دو دست او كشتنى است / ‏بسملش را موجب تاخير چيست
entries.english: An infidel with both hands tied! He is ready for killing: what is the cause of this delay in slaughtering him?”

entries.farsi: آمد آن يك در تفحص در پى‏اش / ‏ديد كافر را به بالاى وى‏اش
entries.english: One of them went after him to investigate: he found the infidel on the top of the Sufi,

entries.farsi: همچو نر بالاى ماده و آن اسير / همچو شيرى خفته بالاى فقير
entries.english: Like a male (animal) upon a female, and the infidel couching upon the dervish like a lion

entries.farsi: دستها بسته همى‏خاييد او / از سر استيزه صوفى را گلو
entries.english: With his hands tied, he was gnawing the Sufi’s throat in obstinate strife.

entries.farsi: گبر مى‏خاييد با دندان گلوش / ‏صوفى افتاده به زير و رفته هوش
entries.english: The infidel was gnawing his throat with his teeth: the Sufi lay beneath, senseless.

entries.farsi: دست بسته گبر همچون گربه‏اى / ‏خسته كرده حلق او بى‏حربه‏اى
entries.english: The pinioned infidel, as a cat, had wounded his throat without a lance.

entries.farsi: نيم كشتش كرده با دندان اسير / ريش او پر خون ز حلق آن فقير
entries.english: The prisoner had half-killed him with his teeth: his beard was soaked in blood from the throat of the dervish.

entries.farsi: همچو تو كز دست نفس بسته دست / ‏همچو آن صوفى شدى بى‏خويش و پست
entries.english: Like you, who under the violence of your pinioned fleshly soul have become as senseless and abject as that Sufi.

entries.farsi: اى شده عاجز ز تلى كيش تو / صد هزاران كوهها در پيش تو
entries.english: O you whose religion is incapable of a single hillock, there are a hundred thousand mountains in front of you.

entries.farsi: زين قدر خر پشته مردى از شكوه / ‏چون روى بر عقبه‏هاى همچو كوه
entries.english: You are dead with fear of a ridge of this size: how will you climb up precipices as a mountain?

entries.farsi: غازيان كشتند كافر را به تيغ / ‏هم در آن ساعت ز حميت بى‏دريغ
entries.english: The warriors, by zeal, at that very instant ruthlessly put the infidel to the sword.

entries.farsi: بر رخ صوفى زدند آب و گالب / ‏تا به هوش آيد ز بى‏هوشى و خواب
entries.english: They sprinkled water and rose-water on the face of the Sufi that he might recover from his unconsciousness and the.

entries.farsi: اللهَّ اللهَّ اين چه حال است اى عزيز / اين چنين بى‏هوش گشتى از چه چيز
entries.english: When he came to himself, he saw the party, and they asked him how it had happened,

entries.farsi: چون به خويش آمد بديد آن قوم را / پس بپرسيدند چون بد ماجرا
entries.english: “God! God! What is the matter, O worshipful one? By what thing were you made so senseless?

entries.farsi: از اسير نيم كشت بسته دست / ‏اين چنين بى‏هوش افتادى و پست
entries.english: Was a half-killed pinioned infidel the cause of your falling into such a senseless and abject plight?”

entries.farsi: گفت چون قصد سرش كردم به خشم / ‏طرفه در من بنگريد آن شوخ چشم
entries.english: He replied, “When I attempted his head in anger, the impudent fellow looked at me queerly.

entries.farsi: چشم را واكرد پهن او سوى من / ‏چشم گردانيد و شد هوشم ز تن
entries.english: He opened his eyes wide at me: he rolled his eyes, and consciousness vanished from my body.

entries.farsi: گردش چشمش مرا لشكر نمود / من ندانم گفت چون پر هول بود
entries.english: The rolling of his eyes seemed to me an army: I cannot describe how terrible it was.

entries.farsi: قصه كوته كن كز آن چشم اين چنين / ‏رفتم از خود اوفتادم بر زمين
entries.english: Cut the story short: from those eyes I became so beside myself and fell to the ground.”

sections.title_fa: نصيحت مبارزان او را كه با اين دل و زهره كه تو دارى كه از كالپيسه شدن چشم كافر اسيرى دست بسته بى‏هوش شوى و دشنه از دست بيفتد زنهار و مالزم مطبخ خانقاه باش و سوى پيكار مرو تا ‏رسوا نشوى
sections.title_en: How the champions counselled him, saying, “Since you have so little heart and stomach

that you are made senseless by the rolling of a captive and pinioned infidel’s eyes, so that the dagger drops from your hand, take heed, take heed! Keep to the kitchen of the Sufi convent and do not go to battle lest you incur public disgrace!”

entries.farsi: قوم گفتندش به پيكار و نبرد / با چنين زهره كه تو دارى مگرد
entries.english: The party said to him, “With such a stomach as you have, do not approach the battle and war.

entries.farsi: چون ز چشم آن اسير بسته دست / ‏غرقه گشتى كشتى تو در شكست
entries.english: Since you were sunk and your ship wrecked by the eye of that pinioned prisoner,

entries.farsi: پس ميان حمله‏ى شيران نر / كه بود با تيغشان چون گوى سر
entries.english: How, then, amidst the onset of the fierce lions, to whose swords the head is like a ball,

entries.farsi: كى توانى كرد در خون آشنا / چون نه اى با جنگ مردان آشنا
entries.english: Can you swim in blood, when you are not familiar with the warfare of men?—

entries.farsi: بس تن بى‏سر كه دارد اضطراب / ‏بس سر بى‏تن به خون بر چون حباب
entries.english: For the pounding noise made by blacksmith’s hammers is banal in comparison with the clang of smiting necks.

entries.farsi: كه ز طاقاطاق گردنها زدن / ‏طاق طاق جامه كوبان ممتهن
entries.english: Many a headless body that is quivering, many a bodiless head on blood, like bubbles.

entries.farsi: اين چنين هوشى كه از موشى پريد / اندر آن صف تيغ چون خواهد كشيد
entries.english: In war, hundreds of death-dealing are drowned under the legs of the horses in death.

entries.farsi: زير دست و پاى اسبان در غزا / صد فنا كن غرقه گشته در فنا
entries.english: How will wits like these, which flew away from a mouse, draw the sword in that battle-line?

entries.farsi: چالش است آن حمزه خوردن نيست اين / نيست حمزه خوردن اينجا تيغ بين
entries.english: It is war, not supping wheat-broth (hamza), that you should turn up your sleeve to sup it.

entries.farsi: ‏حمزه‏اى بايد درين صف آهنين / كار هر نازك دلى نبود قتال
entries.english: It is not supping wheat-broth; here eye the sword! In this battle-line one needs a Hamza of iron.

entries.farsi: جاى تركان هست خانه خانه شو
entries.english: Fighting is not the business of any faint-heart who runs away from a spectre, like a spectre.

entries.farsi: ‏كه گريزد از خيالى چون خيال / كار تركان است نه تركان برو
entries.english: It is the business of Turks (Turkan), not of Tarkan. Begone! Home is the place for Tarkan: go home!”

sections.title_fa: ،حكايت عياضى رحمه اللهَّ كه هفتاد غزو كرده بود سينه برهنه و غزاها كرده بر اميد شهيد شدن چون از آن نوميد شد از جهاد اصغر رو به جهاد اكبر آورد و خلوت گزيد، ناگهان طبل غازيان شنيد ‏نفس از اندرون زنجير مى‏درانيد سوى غزا، و متهم داشتن او نفس خود را در اين رغبت

sections.title_en: Story of Iyadi, may God have mercy on him, who had taken part in seventy campaigns against the infidels and had always fought with his breast bare, in the hope that he might become a martyr; and how, despairing of that, he turned from the Lesser Warfare to the Greater Warfare and adopted the practice of seclusion; and how he suddenly heard the drums of the holy warriors,
and the fleshly soul within him urged him violently to take the field; and how he suspected his fleshly soul in desiring this.

entries.farsi: تن برهنه مى‏شدم در پيش تير / تا يكى تيرى خورم من جاى گير
entries.english: Iyadi said, “Ninety times I came unarmed, that perchance I might be wounded.

entries.farsi: گفت عياضى نود بار آمدم / ‏تن برهنه بو كه زخمى آيدم
entries.english: I went unarmed to meet the arrows, in order that I might receive a deep-seated arrow-wound.

entries.farsi: بر تنم يك جايگه بى‏زخم نيست / ‏اين تنم از تير چون پرويزنى است
entries.english: None but a fortunate martyr attains unto receiving an arrow-wound in the throat or any vital spot.

entries.farsi: تير خوردن بر گلو يا مقتلى / ‏در نيابد جز شهيدى مقبلى
entries.english: No place in my body is without wounds: this body of mine is like a sieve from arrows;

entries.farsi: ليك بر مقتل نيامد تيرها / كار بخت است اين نه جلدى و دها
entries.english: But the arrows never hit a vital spot: this is a matter of luck, not of bravery or cunning.

entries.farsi: چون شهيدى روزى جانم نبود / رفتم اندر خلوت و در چله زود
entries.english: When martyrdom was not the lot of my spirit, I went immediately into seclusion and forty days’ fast.

entries.farsi: در جهاد اكبر افكندم بدن / ‏در رياضت كردن و الغر شدن
entries.english: I threw myself into the Greater Warfare in practicing austerities and becoming lean.

entries.farsi: بانگ طبل غازيان آمد به گوش / ‏كه خراميدند جيش غزو كوش
entries.english: There reached my ear the sound of the drums of the holy warriors; for the hard-fighting army was on the march.

entries.farsi: نفس از باطن مرا آواز داد / كه به گوش حس شنيدم بامداد
entries.english: My fleshly soul cried out to me from within: at morningtide I heard with my sensuous ear,

entries.farsi: خيز هنگام غزا آمد برو / خويش را در غزو كردن كن گرو
entries.english: ‘Arise! It is time to fight. Go; devote yourself to fighting in the holy war!’

entries.farsi: گفتم اى نفس خبيث بى‏وفا / از كجا ميل غزا تو از كجا
entries.english: I answered, ‘O wicked perfidious soul, what have you to do with the desire to fight?

entries.farsi: راست گوى اى نفس كين حيلت‏گرى است / ‏ور نه نفس شهوت از طاعت برى است
entries.english: Tell the truth, O my soul! This is trickery. Else why would you fight?—the lustful soul is quit of obedience.

entries.farsi: گر نگويى راست حمله آرمت / ‏در رياضت سخت‏تر افشارمت
entries.english: Unless you tell the truth, I will attack you; I will squeeze you more painfully in maceration.’

entries.farsi: نفس بانگ آورد آن دم از درون / ‏با فصاحت بى‏دهان اندر فسون
entries.english: Thereupon my soul, mutely eloquent, cried out in guile from within me,

entries.farsi: كه مرا هر روز اينجا مى‏كشى / ‏جان من چون جان گيران مى‏كشى
entries.english: ‘Here you are killing me daily, you are putting my (vital) spirit (on the rack), like the spirits of infidels.

entries.farsi: هيچ كس را نيست از حالم خبر / كه مرا تو مى‏كشى بى‏خواب‏و خور
entries.english: No one is aware of my plight— how you are killing me without sleep and food.

entries.farsi: در دو عالم تو مرايى بوده‏اى / ‏در دو عالم تو چنين بى‏هوده‏اى
entries.english: In war I should escape from the body at one stroke, and the people would see my manly valour and self-sacrifice.’

entries.farsi: در غزا بجهم به يك زخم از بدن / ‏خلق بيند مردى و ايثار من
entries.english: I replied, ‘O wretched soul, a hypocrite you have lived and a hypocrite you will die: what are you!

entries.farsi: گفتم اى نفسك منافق زيستى / ‏هم منافق مى‏مرى تو چيستى
entries.english: In both worlds you have been a hypocrite, in both worlds you are such a worthless creature.’

entries.farsi: نذر كردم كه ز خلوت هيچ من / ‏سر برون نارم چو زنده ست اين بدن
entries.english: I vowed that I would never put my head outside of seclusion, seeing that this body is alive,

entries.farsi: ز انكه در خلوت هر آنچ اين تن كند / نه از براى روى مرد و زن كند
entries.english: Because everything that this body does in seclusion it does with no regard to man or woman.

entries.farsi: اين جهاد اكبر است آن اصغر است / ‏هر دو كار رستم است و حيدر است
entries.english: During seclusion the intention of its movement and rest is for God’s sake only.”

entries.farsi: جنبش و آرامش اندر خلوتش / ‏جز براى حق نباشد نيتش
entries.english: This is the Greater Warfare, and that is the Lesser Warfare: both are work for Rustam and Haydar.

entries.farsi: كار آن كس نيست كاو را عقل و هوش / ‏پرد از تن چون بجنبد دنب موش
entries.english: They are not work for one whose reason and wits fly out of his body when a mouse’s tail moves.

entries.farsi: آن چنان كس را ببايد چون زنان / ‏دور بودن از مصاف و از سنان
entries.english: Such a one must stay, like women, far off from the battle-field and the spears.

entries.farsi: صوفيى آن صوفيى اين اينت حيف / ‏آن ز سوزن كشته اين را طعمه سيف
entries.english: That one a Sufi and this one a Sufi! Here’s a pity! That one is killed by a needle, while the sword is this one’s food.

entries.farsi: بر در و ديوار جسم گل سرشت / ‏حق ز غيرت نقش صد صوفى نبشت
entries.english: He is the figure of a Sufi: he has no soul; accordingly, the Sufis get a bad name from Sufis such as these.

entries.farsi: نقش صوفى باشد او را نيست جان / ‏صوفيان بد نام هم زين صوفيان
entries.english: Upon the door and wall of the body moulded of clay God, in His jealousy, traced the figures of a hundred Sufis,

entries.farsi: تا ز سحر آن نقشها جنبان شود / تا عصاى موسوى پنهان شود
entries.english: To the end that by means of magic those figures should move and that Moses’ rod should be hidden.

entries.farsi: نقشها را مى‏خورد صدق عصا / چشم فرعونى است پر گرد و حصا
entries.english: The truth of the rod swallows up the figures; the Pharaoh-like eye is filled with dust and gravel.

entries.farsi: صوفيى ديگر ميان صف حرب / ‏اندر آمد بيست بار از بهر ضرب
entries.english: Another Sufi entered the battle-line twenty times for the purpose of fighting

entries.farsi: با مسلمانان به كافر وقت كر / وانگشت او با مسلمانان به فر
entries.english: Along with the Moslems when they attacked the infidels; he did not fall back with the Moslems in their retreat.

entries.farsi: تا نميرد تن به يك زخم از گزاف / ‏تا خورد او بيست زخم اندر مصاف
entries.english: He was wounded, but he bandaged the wound which he had received, and once more advanced to the charge and combat,

entries.farsi: زخم خورد و بست زخمى را كه خورد / بار ديگر حمله آورد و نبرد
entries.english: In order that his body might not die cheaply at one blow and that he might receive twenty blows in the battle.

entries.farsi: حيفش آمد كه به زخمى جان دهد / جان ز دست صدق او آسان رهد
entries.english: To him it was anguish that he should give up his soul at one blow and that his soul should escape lightly from the hand of his fortitude.

sections.title_fa: حكايت آن مجاهد كه از هميان سيم هر روز يك درم در خندق انداختى به تفاريق از بهر ستيزه‏ى حرص و آرزوى نفس و وسوسه‏ى نفس كه چون مى‏اندازى به خندق بارى به يك بار بيند از تا ‏خالص يابم كه الياس احدى الراحتين، او گفت كه اين راحت نيز ندهم

sections.title_en: Story of the warrior who every day used to take one dirhem separately from a purse containing silver and throw it into a ditch for the purpose of thwarting the greed and cupidity of his fleshly soul; and how his soul tempted him, saying, “Since you are going to throw into the ditch, at least throw it away all at once, so that I may gain deliverance, for despair is one of the two reliefs”;
and how he replied, “I will not give you this relief either.”

entries.farsi: آن يكى بودش به كف در چل درم / ‏هر شب افكندى يكى در آب يم
